National average fertilizer input rates are calculated by dividing total national nutrient inputs per crop in 2010-2011 as reported by the International Fertilizer Industry Association (Heffer, 2013; IFA, 2013) by total national production of each crop in 2011 as reported by StatsCan (Table 001-0010). Jayasundara et al. (2014) report detailed life cycle inventory models for corn production in Ontario. Since 70% of Canadian corn is pro- duced in Ontario, input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use are derived from this literature source.

The majority of wheat is produced in the prairie provinces (i.e. Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta). Input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use per tonne of wheat produced are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta, but are adjusted to reflect an assumed 3 rather than 4 tonne per hectare yield as per StatsCan for 2011. Similar data for soy production are derived from (S&T)2 (2010), which reports LCI data for the Canadian GHGenius model.

Other significant crop inputs to Canadian egg supply chain feeds include barley, peas and canola. For barley, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). Yields are adjusted to reflect the 2012 Canadian average as reported by StatsCan (2.9 tonnes/ha). For peas, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). The Canadian average yield based on StatsCan data from 2012 (2.3 tonnes/ha) is employed. Saskatchewan and Alberta account for 98% of Canadian pea production. For canola, input data per tonne are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta. Together, Alberta, Man- itoba and Saskatchewan account for 98% of Canadian canola pro- duction. Again, a Canadian average yield for 2012 based on StatsCan data is assumed (1.6 tonnes/ha).

Since organic egg production was among the housing systems modeled, it was also necessary to develop life cycle inventory data models for organic feed input production. Four significant raw materials were reported as inputs to organic feeds. These were organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. Pelletier et al. (2008) reported life cycle inventory data for major Canadian field crops, including organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. These life cycle inventory models were adopted for use in the current analysis.

Field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate and carbon dioxide (from urea fertilizers) emissions were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols using default emission fac- tors. A 2.9% surplus phosphate emission rate was assumed following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizers and pesticides were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, and all seed inputs 100 km by truck.

Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were defined based on data from the International Fertilizer Industry Association (IFA, 2013). Life cycle inventory data models for individual fertilizer in- puts were taken from the EcoInvent database. These models represent average production conditions in Europe.
Processing of wheat, soy, corn and canola was based on in- ventory data reported by Pelletier et al. (2009; 2010a,b; 2014a,b).

A variety of materials derived from rendered livestock products were reported as inputs to feeds utilized in the layer supply chain. These include both fat and meat/bone meal products from cattle, swine and poultry as well as poultry feather meal. The life cycle inventory models for beef and swine production employed in the current study were adopted from studies by Pelletier and col- leagues of contemporary production systems in the mid-western

United States (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with all US supply chain inputs replaced with equivalent Canadian inputs. Rendered poultry products were assumed to be derived from spent hens as modeled in the current analysis. Data for the production of ruminant and porcine meat and bone meal and fat follow Pelletier et al. (2014b, 2010a, 2010b, 2009). Other inputs to feed in the Canadian egg supply chain include sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids and other pre-mixes and supplements. Inventory data for these inputs were largely derived from the EcoInvent database. All mineral inputs were modeled as salt. Inventory data for the production of DL- methionine from Binder (2003) was used in proxy for all amino acid inputs. As pre-mixes and supplements may vary widely in composition and detailed composition data were not available, they were excluded from the analysis.

2.2.1.4. Compound feed production. Separate, consumption- weighted average feed formulations were modeled for each step of the egg supply chain. For pullets, one conventional and one organic feed formulation were modeled. For layers, a single, feed formulation was modeled for conventional layer feed. This feed was assumed to be used in conventional cage, enriched, free run and free range systems. A second organic layer feed formulation was modeled also, again based on consumption-weighted producer reported data.

2.2.1.5. Egg processing, packaging, and further processing. Survey data were obtained from 10 Canadian shell egg processing (i.e. washing, grading and packaging) facilities collectively repre- senting 157,024,543 dozen processed eggs in 2012.
Due to insufficient data regarding packaging of eggs and egg products, inventory data were adopted from Pelletier et al. (2013) for egg supply chains in the mid-western United States.

Survey data were obtained from only one egg breaking and further processing facility in Canada. For confidentiality reasons, it is not possible to report these data. For this reason, life cycle in- ventory data for egg breaking and production of pasteurized liquid egg were derived from Pelletier et al. (2013). These data were collected from six egg breaking and further processing facilities in the Midwestern United States in 2009, and represent 372,443,325 dozen eggs broken. Data are presented for pasteurized, liquid whole eggs only.

2.2.1.6. Energy carriers and mixes. Provincial electricity life cycle inventory models are taken from the EcoInvent database. Production-weighted average electricity mixes were modeled for each supply chain stage, including for crop input production, feed input processing, and breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, grading, and breaking facilities. The same primary energy carrier processes used in the EcoIn- vent Canadian provincial electricity mix processes are employed wherever otherwise required in the life cycle inventory models. This includes models for natural gas, liquid propane gas (lpg), diesel, fuel oil, and gasoline. The latter data are based on European models, and should be replaced with Canadian data once such data become available. For layer facilities, one production-weighted average housing energy mix was developed for conventional cage and enriched cage production, one for free run production, and one for free range and organic production.

2.2.1.7. Transportation. Production-weighted, producer-reported transport modes and distances were applied wherever possible. For all truck transport, the EcoInvent inventory model representing average European fleet conditions for trucks greater than 16 tonnes

was applied. This model is likely conservative in the Canadian context given the long-distance hauling characteristic of Canadian road freight. Similarly, all tractor transport was modeled using the average EcoInvent inventory model for a tractor and trailer.
22: <header>2.2.1. Data sources and assumptions </header>

For the purpose of the current analysis, foreground system data are those directly collected from supply chain participants in the study. Foreground system data were collected from breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, shell egg processor, and breaker facilities via survey (see Supplemental Information). Efforts were made to ensure a representative sample by province, housing system type and farm size. In total, the 88 surveys collected from 104 layer fa- cilities represented roughly 10% of all laying hens nation-wide in 2012 (for sample sizes and representation for each housing system type, see Table S9).

Alberta Agriculture and Rural Development (AARD) previously commissioned the consulting firm, Quantis, to conduct an LCA study of egg production in Alberta (Courchesne et al., 2013). In order to minimize reporting burdens for Alberta egg farmers, the inventory data collected by Quantis and Egg Farmers of Alberta were adopted for use in the current analysis. For consistency with the life cycle inventory data collected in the current study for other provinces, a short-version survey was administered to those egg farmers that previously supplied data for the AARD study (see Supplementary Information) in order to collect the additional necessary data.

Surveys were administered by provincial egg boards and their field officers. Completed surveys were coded such that follow-up questions could be directed to data providers whilst ensuring data source confidentiality. All data were collected under guarantee of non-disclosure with respect to individual facilities. In the absence of adequate Canadian data, feed milling energy input data

were instead derived from recent LCA studies of the US egg in- dustry (Pelletier et al., 2014b, 2013). These data were collected from eleven reporting facilities in the United States in 2010 and repre- sent a total production of 2,679,405 tonnes of pullet and layer feed. Feed formulations were derived from directly collected, producer- reported survey data.

Certain practices may vary between facilities. For example, manure management strategies may entail liquid or solid manure handling and storage, variable storage times, and varied uses of the manure. In such cases, production-weighted average processes were defined for each housing system type based on directly collected data. In light of low data resolution/quality with respect to reported quantities of manure removed from facilities, a standard excretion rate was assumed for both pullet and layer facilities based on Pelletier et al. (2014b), and scaled relative to reported feed conversion efficiencies. For mass of manure handled (i.e. for the purpose of quantifying manure transport), the following assump- tions were also implemented:

<list>for liquid manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to the mass of manure excreted 
for dry manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to 50% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to uncovered storage, and 33% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to covered storage 
for free range and organic systems, it was assumed that 50% of manure was excreted outdoors and was not subject to handling</list>

Since egg supply chain activities are not evenly distributed be- tween provinces and each province has characteristic electricity grid mixes, national average mixes for each supply chain stage were developed based on Canadian statistical data for provincial egg sector activity volumes and Canadian provincial electricity mixes as available in the EcoInvent (2013) database.

Background system data for the current analysis include the production and supply of feed inputs, water, energy carriers, packaging and transport modes. Background system data other than feed input production and processing models were derived from the EcoInvent database, but modified where possible to best approximate Canadian conditions by using Canadian energy inputs/ mixes.

2.2.1.1. National and provincial layer supply chain statistics. For hatcheries, hatch rates were calculated for each province by dividing total eggs set in 2012 by chicks hatched. Eggs required per female chick set for pullet production was calculated using Agri- culture and Agri-Food Canada Poultry Section data tables for "eggs set for egg production," "chicks hatched for egg production," and "hatchery review: chicks destroyed" (Tables S1-S2).

For pullet facilities, total pullets placed for egg production is based on "Hatchery Review: Eggs Set and Chicks Hatched - Place- ment of Chicks" based on data from the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA), Institut de la Statistique du Québec (ISQ) and the Canadian Hatching Egg Producers (CHEP), as compiled by AAFC, AID, Poultry Section (Table S3).

The number of layers in each province in 2012 and rate-of-lay by province are based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada (Table S4). National egg production (1000 dozens) by province is based on StatsCan table 003-0020 (Production and disposition of eggs) (Table S5). The distribution of egg grades by province are taken from information collated by EFC from provincial egg boards, accessed via the Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada website. Egg grade size is taken from Schedule 3, Table 2 of the Egg Regulation.

Mass of eggs in each grade are defined in the Regulation as a minimum value, with increments of 7 g between sizes for Grade A eggs. Mass is not provided for other grades. Mass for each size of Grade A eggs (i.e. Jumbo, Extra Large, Large, Medium, Small, Peewee) was assumed to be the minimum size +3.5 g for each size (for PW, this was mass for Small minus 3.5 g). Based on national average grade distribution data from EFC, the mass of the other grades (i.e. B, C and NR) was calculated as the production weighted average of the national grade A size distribution. Egg production volume (in tonnes) was subsequently calculated by multiplying total production (in 1000 dozens) for each province in 2012 by the % size distribution and mass for each province (Table S6). Data for eggs broken for further processing by province were not available, hence breaking was assumed to occur in the province in which the eggs were produced.

Based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada, there were 19,981,902 layers housed in the five housing systems considered in this study in 2012 (note that this excludes 0.09% of Canadian layers which were group classified as "Aviary, Free Run, Free Range, Organic" rather than attributed to a specific housing system). Conventional cage systems accounted for the majority (92.3%) of layers. Survey data directly collected from laying facilities for this study represented 10.7% of the overall layer population. Propor- tional data representation was lowest for conventional cage hous- ing (9.3% of layers in conventional cages in Canada in 2012), and ranged from 20-40% for the other housing systems considered (Table S7). Given the much larger population of conventional cage layers and the large number of surveys collected from conventional cage facilities, this sample size is considered acceptable for the study. Similarly, the larger proportion of representation for the alternative housing systems is considered desirable in light of the much smaller number of layers housed in these systems in Canada (i.e. a greater proportional representation is necessary to achieve a similar degree of statistical power).

2.2.1.2. Modeling N, P and methane emissions from poultry manure. A nutrient mass balance model based on feed composition (prox- imate compositions of individual feed inputs were derived from www.feedipedia.org) and assuming that: (1) 2.2% of hen body mass is nitrogen and 0.6% is phosphorus and (2) eggs contain 1.7% ni- trogen and 0.21% phosphorus (following Koelsch (2007)) was used to calculate nitrogen and phosphorus excretion rates. In turn, estimated nitrogen excretion rates were used to calculate direct nitrous oxide, ammonia and nitric oxide emissions from manure management and indirect nitrous oxide emissions from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions following IPCC (2006) Tier 2 protocols and default emission factors at time of deposition, storage and application. These calculations took into account the propor- tion of each manure management system employed (i.e. weighted average emission factors are calculated). For losses of N due to ni- trate leaching during manure storage, IPCC suggests a range of 1-20%. The value employed in the current analysis is 20% for un- covered manure storage and 0% for covered manure storage. Following Dalgaard et al. (2008), phosphorus emissions were calculated based on an assumed 2.9% run-off rate at time of application of manure to agricultural lands. Methane emissions from manure management were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier I protocols, taking into account the proportions and resi- dency time of manure in dry and wet manure management sys- items. Methane emission factors for layers (0.03 kg and 1.2 kg CH4/ head/year for hens in dry and wet manure management systems, respectively) were applied for calculating emissions from breeder flock and pullet facilities.
2.2.1.3. Modeling feed input supply chains. Thirty distinct feed

inputs were reported as contributing to breeder flock, pullet and layer feeds. Of these, the three major inputs to feeds for egg pro- duction supply chains are corn, wheat and soybeans. The pro- portions of these inputs used in each province varies, as do the underpinning inputs of fertilizers, energy, pesticides, and other resources.

For the purpose of this study, Canadian-average models are developed for wheat, corn and soy, based on the following data sources and assumptions. Jayasundara et al. (2014) report detailed life cycle inventory models for corn production in Ontario. Since 70% of Canadian corn is pro- duced in Ontario, input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use are derived from this literature source.

The majority of wheat is produced in the prairie provinces (i.e. Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta). Input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use per tonne of wheat produced are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta, but are adjusted to reflect an assumed 3 rather than 4 tonne per hectare yield as per StatsCan for 2011. Similar data for soy production are derived from (S&T)2 (2010), which reports LCI data for the Canadian GHGenius model.

Other significant crop inputs to Canadian egg supply chain feeds include barley, peas and canola. For barley, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). Yields are adjusted to reflect the 2012 Canadian average as reported by StatsCan (2.9 tonnes/ha). For peas, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). The Canadian average yield based on StatsCan data from 2012 (2.3 tonnes/ha) is employed. Saskatchewan and Alberta account for 98% of Canadian pea production. For canola, input data per tonne are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta. Together, Alberta, Man- itoba and Saskatchewan account for 98% of Canadian canola pro- duction. Again, a Canadian average yield for 2012 based on StatsCan data is assumed (1.6 tonnes/ha).

Since organic egg production was among the housing systems modeled, it was also necessary to develop life cycle inventory data models for organic feed input production. Four significant raw materials were reported as inputs to organic feeds. These were organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. Pelletier et al. (2008) reported life cycle inventory data for major Canadian field crops, including organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. These life cycle inventory models were adopted for use in the current analysis.

Field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate and carbon dioxide (from urea fertilizers) emissions were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols using default emission fac- tors. A 2.9% surplus phosphate emission rate was assumed following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizers and pesticides were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, and all seed inputs 100 km by truck.

Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were defined based on data from the International Fertilizer Industry Association (IFA, 2013). Life cycle inventory data models for individual fertilizer in- puts were taken from the EcoInvent database. These models represent average production conditions in Europe.
Processing of wheat, soy, corn and canola was based on in- ventory data reported by Pelletier et al. (2009; 2010a,b; 2014a,b).

A variety of materials derived from rendered livestock products were reported as inputs to feeds utilized in the layer supply chain. These include both fat and meat/bone meal products from cattle, swine and poultry as well as poultry feather meal. The life cycle inventory models for beef and swine production employed in the current study were adopted from studies by Pelletier and col- leagues of contemporary production systems in the mid-western

United States (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with all US supply chain inputs replaced with equivalent Canadian inputs. Rendered poultry products were assumed to be derived from spent hens as modeled in the current analysis. Data for the production of ruminant and porcine meat and bone meal and fat follow Pelletier et al. (2014b, 2010a, 2010b, 2009). (2008) reported life cycle inventory data for major Canadian field crops, including organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. These life cycle inventory models were adopted for use in the current analysis.

Field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate and carbon dioxide (from urea fertilizers) emissions were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols using default emission fac- tors. A 2.9% surplus phosphate emission rate was assumed following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizers and pesticides were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, and all seed inputs 100 km by truck.

Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were defined based on data from the International Fertilizer Industry Association (IFA, 2013). Life cycle inventory data models for individual fertilizer in- puts were taken from the EcoInvent database. These models represent average production conditions in Europe.
Processing of wheat, soy, corn and canola was based on in- ventory data reported by Pelletier et al. (2009; 2010a,b; 2014a,b).

A variety of materials derived from rendered livestock products were reported as inputs to feeds utilized in the layer supply chain. These include both fat and meat/bone meal products from cattle, swine and poultry as well as poultry feather meal. The life cycle inventory models for beef and swine production employed in the current study were adopted from studies by Pelletier and col- leagues of contemporary production systems in the mid-western

United States (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with all US supply chain inputs replaced with equivalent Canadian inputs. Rendered poultry products were assumed to be derived from spent hens as modeled in the current analysis. Data for the production of ruminant and porcine meat and bone meal and fat follow Pelletier et al. (2014b, 2010a, 2010b, 2009). Other inputs to feed in the Canadian egg supply chain include sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids and other pre-mixes and supplements. Inventory data for these inputs were largely derived from the EcoInvent database. All mineral inputs were modeled as salt. Inventory data for the production of DL- methionine from Binder (2003) was used in proxy for all amino acid inputs. As pre-mixes and supplements may vary widely in composition and detailed composition data were not available, they were excluded from the analysis.

2.2.1.4. Compound feed production. Separate, consumption- weighted average feed formulations were modeled for each step of the egg supply chain. For pullets, one conventional and one organic feed formulation were modeled. For layers, a single, feed formulation was modeled for conventional layer feed. This feed was assumed to be used in conventional cage, enriched, free run and free range systems. A second organic layer feed formulation was modeled also, again based on consumption-weighted producer reported data.

2.2.1.5. Egg processing, packaging, and further processing. Survey data were obtained from 10 Canadian shell egg processing (i.e. washing, grading and packaging) facilities collectively repre- senting 157,024,543 dozen processed eggs in 2012.
Due to insufficient data regarding packaging of eggs and egg products, inventory data were adopted from Pelletier et al. (2013) for egg supply chains in the mid-western United States.

Survey data were obtained from only one egg breaking and further processing facility in Canada. For confidentiality reasons, it is not possible to report these data. For this reason, life cycle in- ventory data for egg breaking and production of pasteurized liquid egg were derived from Pelletier et al. (2013). These data were collected from six egg breaking and further processing facilities in the Midwestern United States in 2009, and represent 372,443,325 dozen eggs broken. Data are presented for pasteurized, liquid whole eggs only.

2.2.1.6. Energy carriers and mixes. Provincial electricity life cycle inventory models are taken from the EcoInvent database. Production-weighted average electricity mixes were modeled for each supply chain stage, including for crop input production, feed input processing, and breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, grading, and breaking facilities. The same primary energy carrier processes used in the EcoIn- vent Canadian provincial electricity mix processes are employed wherever otherwise required in the life cycle inventory models. This includes models for natural gas, liquid propane gas (lpg), diesel, fuel oil, and gasoline. The latter data are based on European models, and should be replaced with Canadian data once such data become available. For layer facilities, one production-weighted average housing energy mix was developed for conventional cage and enriched cage production, one for free run production, and one for free range and organic production.

2.2.1.7. Transportation. Production-weighted, producer-reported transport modes and distances were applied wherever possible. For all truck transport, the EcoInvent inventory model representing average European fleet conditions for trucks greater than 16 tonnes

was applied. This model is likely conservative in the Canadian context given the long-distance hauling characteristic of Canadian road freight. Similarly, all tractor transport was modeled using the average EcoInvent inventory model for a tractor and trailer.
24: <header>2.2.1. Data sources and assumptions </header>

For the purpose of the current analysis, foreground system data are those directly collected from supply chain participants in the study. Foreground system data were collected from breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, shell egg processor, and breaker facilities via survey (see Supplemental Information). Efforts were made to ensure a representative sample by province, housing system type and farm size. In total, the 88 surveys collected from 104 layer fa- cilities represented roughly 10% of all laying hens nation-wide in 2012 (for sample sizes and representation for each housing system type, see Table S9).

Alberta Agriculture and Rural Development (AARD) previously commissioned the consulting firm, Quantis, to conduct an LCA study of egg production in Alberta (Courchesne et al., 2013). In order to minimize reporting burdens for Alberta egg farmers, the inventory data collected by Quantis and Egg Farmers of Alberta were adopted for use in the current analysis. For consistency with the life cycle inventory data collected in the current study for other provinces, a short-version survey was administered to those egg farmers that previously supplied data for the AARD study (see Supplementary Information) in order to collect the additional necessary data.

Surveys were administered by provincial egg boards and their field officers. Completed surveys were coded such that follow-up questions could be directed to data providers whilst ensuring data source confidentiality. All data were collected under guarantee of non-disclosure with respect to individual facilities. In the absence of adequate Canadian data, feed milling energy input data

were instead derived from recent LCA studies of the US egg in- dustry (Pelletier et al., 2014b, 2013). These data were collected from eleven reporting facilities in the United States in 2010 and repre- sent a total production of 2,679,405 tonnes of pullet and layer feed. Feed formulations were derived from directly collected, producer- reported survey data.

Certain practices may vary between facilities. For example, manure management strategies may entail liquid or solid manure handling and storage, variable storage times, and varied uses of the manure. In such cases, production-weighted average processes were defined for each housing system type based on directly collected data. In light of low data resolution/quality with respect to reported quantities of manure removed from facilities, a standard excretion rate was assumed for both pullet and layer facilities based on Pelletier et al. (2014b), and scaled relative to reported feed conversion efficiencies. For mass of manure handled (i.e. for the purpose of quantifying manure transport), the following assump- tions were also implemented:

<list>for liquid manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to the mass of manure excreted 
for dry manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to 50% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to uncovered storage, and 33% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to covered storage 
for free range and organic systems, it was assumed that 50% of manure was excreted outdoors and was not subject to handling</list>

Since egg supply chain activities are not evenly distributed be- tween provinces and each province has characteristic electricity grid mixes, national average mixes for each supply chain stage were developed based on Canadian statistical data for provincial egg sector activity volumes and Canadian provincial electricity mixes as available in the EcoInvent (2013) database.

Background system data for the current analysis include the production and supply of feed inputs, water, energy carriers, packaging and transport modes. Background system data other than feed input production and processing models were derived from the EcoInvent database, but modified where possible to best approximate Canadian conditions by using Canadian energy inputs/ mixes.

2.2.1.1. National and provincial layer supply chain statistics. For hatcheries, hatch rates were calculated for each province by dividing total eggs set in 2012 by chicks hatched. Eggs required per female chick set for pullet production was calculated using Agri- culture and Agri-Food Canada Poultry Section data tables for "eggs set for egg production," "chicks hatched for egg production," and "hatchery review: chicks destroyed" (Tables S1-S2).

For pullet facilities, total pullets placed for egg production is based on "Hatchery Review: Eggs Set and Chicks Hatched - Place- ment of Chicks" based on data from the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA), Institut de la Statistique du Québec (ISQ) and the Canadian Hatching Egg Producers (CHEP), as compiled by AAFC, AID, Poultry Section (Table S3).

The number of layers in each province in 2012 and rate-of-lay by province are based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada (Table S4). National egg production (1000 dozens) by province is based on StatsCan table 003-0020 (Production and disposition of eggs) (Table S5). The distribution of egg grades by province are taken from information collated by EFC from provincial egg boards, accessed via the Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada website. Egg grade size is taken from Schedule 3, Table 2 of the Egg Regulation.

Mass of eggs in each grade are defined in the Regulation as a minimum value, with increments of 7 g between sizes for Grade A eggs. Mass is not provided for other grades. Mass for each size of Grade A eggs (i.e. Jumbo, Extra Large, Large, Medium, Small, Peewee) was assumed to be the minimum size +3.5 g for each size (for PW, this was mass for Small minus 3.5 g). Based on national average grade distribution data from EFC, the mass of the other grades (i.e. B, C and NR) was calculated as the production weighted average of the national grade A size distribution. Egg production volume (in tonnes) was subsequently calculated by multiplying total production (in 1000 dozens) for each province in 2012 by the % size distribution and mass for each province (Table S6). Data for eggs broken for further processing by province were not available, hence breaking was assumed to occur in the province in which the eggs were produced.

Based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada, there were 19,981,902 layers housed in the five housing systems considered in this study in 2012 (note that this excludes 0.09% of Canadian layers which were group classified as "Aviary, Free Run, Free Range, Organic" rather than attributed to a specific housing system). Conventional cage systems accounted for the majority (92.3%) of layers. Survey data directly collected from laying facilities for this study represented 10.7% of the overall layer population. Propor- tional data representation was lowest for conventional cage hous- ing (9.3% of layers in conventional cages in Canada in 2012), and ranged from 20-40% for the other housing systems considered (Table S7). Given the much larger population of conventional cage layers and the large number of surveys collected from conventional cage facilities, this sample size is considered acceptable for the study. Similarly, the larger proportion of representation for the alternative housing systems is considered desirable in light of the much smaller number of layers housed in these systems in Canada (i.e. a greater proportional representation is necessary to achieve a similar degree of statistical power).

2.2.1.2. Modeling N, P and methane emissions from poultry manure. A nutrient mass balance model based on feed composition (prox- imate compositions of individual feed inputs were derived from www.feedipedia.org) and assuming that: (1) 2.2% of hen body mass is nitrogen and 0.6% is phosphorus and (2) eggs contain 1.7% ni- trogen and 0.21% phosphorus (following Koelsch (2007)) was used to calculate nitrogen and phosphorus excretion rates. In turn, estimated nitrogen excretion rates were used to calculate direct nitrous oxide, ammonia and nitric oxide emissions from manure management and indirect nitrous oxide emissions from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions following IPCC (2006) Tier 2 protocols and default emission factors at time of deposition, storage and application. These calculations took into account the propor- tion of each manure management system employed (i.e. weighted average emission factors are calculated). For losses of N due to ni- trate leaching during manure storage, IPCC suggests a range of 1-20%. The value employed in the current analysis is 20% for un- covered manure storage and 0% for covered manure storage. Following Dalgaard et al. (2008), phosphorus emissions were calculated based on an assumed 2.9% run-off rate at time of application of manure to agricultural lands. Methane emissions from manure management were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier I protocols, taking into account the proportions and resi- dency time of manure in dry and wet manure management sys- items. Methane emission factors for layers (0.03 kg and 1.2 kg CH4/ head/year for hens in dry and wet manure management systems, respectively) were applied for calculating emissions from breeder flock and pullet facilities.
2.2.1.3. Modeling feed input supply chains. Thirty distinct feed

inputs were reported as contributing to breeder flock, pullet and layer feeds. Of these, the three major inputs to feeds for egg pro- duction supply chains are corn, wheat and soybeans. The pro- portions of these inputs used in each province varies, as do the underpinning inputs of fertilizers, energy, pesticides, and other resources.

For the purpose of this study, Canadian-average models are developed for wheat, corn and soy, based on the following data sources and assumptions. National average fertilizer input rates are calculated by dividing total national nutrient inputs per crop in 2010-2011 as reported by the International Fertilizer Industry Association (Heffer, 2013; IFA, 2013) by total national production of each crop in 2011 as reported by StatsCan (Table 001-0010). Jayasundara et al. (2014) report detailed life cycle inventory models for corn production in Ontario. Since 70% of Canadian corn is pro- duced in Ontario, input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use are derived from this literature source.

The majority of wheat is produced in the prairie provinces (i.e. Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta). Input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use per tonne of wheat produced are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta, but are adjusted to reflect an assumed 3 rather than 4 tonne per hectare yield as per StatsCan for 2011. Similar data for soy production are derived from (S&T)2 (2010), which reports LCI data for the Canadian GHGenius model.

Other significant crop inputs to Canadian egg supply chain feeds include barley, peas and canola. For barley, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). Yields are adjusted to reflect the 2012 Canadian average as reported by StatsCan (2.9 tonnes/ha). For peas, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). The Canadian average yield based on StatsCan data from 2012 (2.3 tonnes/ha) is employed. Saskatchewan and Alberta account for 98% of Canadian pea production. For canola, input data per tonne are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta. Together, Alberta, Man- itoba and Saskatchewan account for 98% of Canadian canola pro- duction. Again, a Canadian average yield for 2012 based on StatsCan data is assumed (1.6 tonnes/ha).

Since organic egg production was among the housing systems modeled, it was also necessary to develop life cycle inventory data models for organic feed input production. Four significant raw materials were reported as inputs to organic feeds. These were organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. Pelletier et al. (2008) reported life cycle inventory data for major Canadian field crops, including organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. These life cycle inventory models were adopted for use in the current analysis.

Field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate and carbon dioxide (from urea fertilizers) emissions were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols using default emission fac- tors. A 2.9% surplus phosphate emission rate was assumed following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizers and pesticides were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, and all seed inputs 100 km by truck.

Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were defined based on data from the International Fertilizer Industry Association (IFA, 2013). Life cycle inventory data models for individual fertilizer in- puts were taken from the EcoInvent database. These models represent average production conditions in Europe.
Processing of wheat, soy, corn and canola was based on in- ventory data reported by Pelletier et al. (2009; 2010a,b; 2014a,b).

A variety of materials derived from rendered livestock products were reported as inputs to feeds utilized in the layer supply chain. These include both fat and meat/bone meal products from cattle, swine and poultry as well as poultry feather meal. The life cycle inventory models for beef and swine production employed in the current study were adopted from studies by Pelletier and col- leagues of contemporary production systems in the mid-western

United States (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with all US supply chain inputs replaced with equivalent Canadian inputs. Rendered poultry products were assumed to be derived from spent hens as modeled in the current analysis. Data for the production of ruminant and porcine meat and bone meal and fat follow Pelletier et al. (2014b, 2010a, 2010b, 2009). Other inputs to feed in the Canadian egg supply chain include sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids and other pre-mixes and supplements. Inventory data for these inputs were largely derived from the EcoInvent database. All mineral inputs were modeled as salt. Inventory data for the production of DL- methionine from Binder (2003) was used in proxy for all amino acid inputs. As pre-mixes and supplements may vary widely in composition and detailed composition data were not available, they were excluded from the analysis.

2.2.1.4. Compound feed production. Separate, consumption- weighted average feed formulations were modeled for each step of the egg supply chain. For pullets, one conventional and one organic feed formulation were modeled. For layers, a single, feed formulation was modeled for conventional layer feed. This feed was assumed to be used in conventional cage, enriched, free run and free range systems. A second organic layer feed formulation was modeled also, again based on consumption-weighted producer reported data.

2.2.1.5. Egg processing, packaging, and further processing. Survey data were obtained from 10 Canadian shell egg processing (i.e. washing, grading and packaging) facilities collectively repre- senting 157,024,543 dozen processed eggs in 2012.
Due to insufficient data regarding packaging of eggs and egg products, inventory data were adopted from Pelletier et al. (2013) for egg supply chains in the mid-western United States.

Survey data were obtained from only one egg breaking and further processing facility in Canada. For confidentiality reasons, it is not possible to report these data. For this reason, life cycle in- ventory data for egg breaking and production of pasteurized liquid egg were derived from Pelletier et al. (2013). These data were collected from six egg breaking and further processing facilities in the Midwestern United States in 2009, and represent 372,443,325 dozen eggs broken. Data are presented for pasteurized, liquid whole eggs only.

2.2.1.6. Energy carriers and mixes. Provincial electricity life cycle inventory models are taken from the EcoInvent database. Production-weighted average electricity mixes were modeled for each supply chain stage, including for crop input production, feed input processing, and breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, grading, and breaking facilities. The same primary energy carrier processes used in the EcoIn- vent Canadian provincial electricity mix processes are employed wherever otherwise required in the life cycle inventory models. This includes models for natural gas, liquid propane gas (lpg), diesel, fuel oil, and gasoline. The latter data are based on European models, and should be replaced with Canadian data once such data become available. For layer facilities, one production-weighted average housing energy mix was developed for conventional cage and enriched cage production, one for free run production, and one for free range and organic production.

2.2.1.7. Transportation. Production-weighted, producer-reported transport modes and distances were applied wherever possible. For all truck transport, the EcoInvent inventory model representing average European fleet conditions for trucks greater than 16 tonnes

was applied. This model is likely conservative in the Canadian context given the long-distance hauling characteristic of Canadian road freight. Similarly, all tractor transport was modeled using the average EcoInvent inventory model for a tractor and trailer.

As pre-mixes and supplements may vary widely in composition and detailed composition data were not available, they were excluded from the analysis.

2.2.1.4. Compound feed production. Separate, consumption- weighted average feed formulations were modeled for each step of the egg supply chain. For pullets, one conventional and one organic feed formulation were modeled. For layers, a single, feed formulation was modeled for conventional layer feed. This feed was assumed to be used in conventional cage, enriched, free run and free range systems. A second organic layer feed formulation was modeled also, again based on consumption-weighted producer reported data.

2.2.1.5. Egg processing, packaging, and further processing. Survey data were obtained from 10 Canadian shell egg processing (i.e. washing, grading and packaging) facilities collectively repre- senting 157,024,543 dozen processed eggs in 2012.
Due to insufficient data regarding packaging of eggs and egg products, inventory data were adopted from Pelletier et al. (2013) for egg supply chains in the mid-western United States.

Survey data were obtained from only one egg breaking and further processing facility in Canada. For confidentiality reasons, it is not possible to report these data. For this reason, life cycle in- ventory data for egg breaking and production of pasteurized liquid egg were derived from Pelletier et al. (2013). These data were collected from six egg breaking and further processing facilities in the Midwestern United States in 2009, and represent 372,443,325 dozen eggs broken. Data are presented for pasteurized, liquid whole eggs only.

2.2.1.6. Energy carriers and mixes. Provincial electricity life cycle inventory models are taken from the EcoInvent database. Production-weighted average electricity mixes were modeled for each supply chain stage, including for crop input production, feed input processing, and breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, grading, and breaking facilities. The same primary energy carrier processes used in the EcoIn- vent Canadian provincial electricity mix processes are employed wherever otherwise required in the life cycle inventory models. This includes models for natural gas, liquid propane gas (lpg), diesel, fuel oil, and gasoline. The latter data are based on European models, and should be replaced with Canadian data once such data become available. For layer facilities, one production-weighted average housing energy mix was developed for conventional cage and enriched cage production, one for free run production, and one for free range and organic production.

2.2.1.7. Transportation. Production-weighted, producer-reported transport modes and distances were applied wherever possible. For all truck transport, the EcoInvent inventory model representing average European fleet conditions for trucks greater than 16 tonnes

was applied. This model is likely conservative in the Canadian context given the long-distance hauling characteristic of Canadian road freight. Similarly, all tractor transport was modeled using the average EcoInvent inventory model for a tractor and trailer.
26: <header>2.2.1. Data sources and assumptions </header>

For the purpose of the current analysis, foreground system data are those directly collected from supply chain participants in the study. Foreground system data were collected from breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, shell egg processor, and breaker facilities via survey (see Supplemental Information). Efforts were made to ensure a representative sample by province, housing system type and farm size. In total, the 88 surveys collected from 104 layer fa- cilities represented roughly 10% of all laying hens nation-wide in 2012 (for sample sizes and representation for each housing system type, see Table S9).

Alberta Agriculture and Rural Development (AARD) previously commissioned the consulting firm, Quantis, to conduct an LCA study of egg production in Alberta (Courchesne et al., 2013). In order to minimize reporting burdens for Alberta egg farmers, the inventory data collected by Quantis and Egg Farmers of Alberta were adopted for use in the current analysis. For consistency with the life cycle inventory data collected in the current study for other provinces, a short-version survey was administered to those egg farmers that previously supplied data for the AARD study (see Supplementary Information) in order to collect the additional necessary data.

Surveys were administered by provincial egg boards and their field officers. Completed surveys were coded such that follow-up questions could be directed to data providers whilst ensuring data source confidentiality. All data were collected under guarantee of non-disclosure with respect to individual facilities. In the absence of adequate Canadian data, feed milling energy input data

were instead derived from recent LCA studies of the US egg in- dustry (Pelletier et al., 2014b, 2013). These data were collected from eleven reporting facilities in the United States in 2010 and repre- sent a total production of 2,679,405 tonnes of pullet and layer feed. Feed formulations were derived from directly collected, producer- reported survey data.

Certain practices may vary between facilities. For example, manure management strategies may entail liquid or solid manure handling and storage, variable storage times, and varied uses of the manure. In such cases, production-weighted average processes were defined for each housing system type based on directly collected data. In light of low data resolution/quality with respect to reported quantities of manure removed from facilities, a standard excretion rate was assumed for both pullet and layer facilities based on Pelletier et al. (2014b), and scaled relative to reported feed conversion efficiencies. For mass of manure handled (i.e. for the purpose of quantifying manure transport), the following assump- tions were also implemented:

<list>for liquid manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to the mass of manure excreted 
for dry manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to 50% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to uncovered storage, and 33% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to covered storage 
for free range and organic systems, it was assumed that 50% of manure was excreted outdoors and was not subject to handling</list>

Since egg supply chain activities are not evenly distributed be- tween provinces and each province has characteristic electricity grid mixes, national average mixes for each supply chain stage were developed based on Canadian statistical data for provincial egg sector activity volumes and Canadian provincial electricity mixes as available in the EcoInvent (2013) database.

Background system data for the current analysis include the production and supply of feed inputs, water, energy carriers, packaging and transport modes. Background system data other than feed input production and processing models were derived from the EcoInvent database, but modified where possible to best approximate Canadian conditions by using Canadian energy inputs/ mixes.

2.2.1.1. National and provincial layer supply chain statistics. For hatcheries, hatch rates were calculated for each province by dividing total eggs set in 2012 by chicks hatched. Eggs required per female chick set for pullet production was calculated using Agri- culture and Agri-Food Canada Poultry Section data tables for "eggs set for egg production," "chicks hatched for egg production," and "hatchery review: chicks destroyed" (Tables S1-S2).

For pullet facilities, total pullets placed for egg production is based on "Hatchery Review: Eggs Set and Chicks Hatched - Place- ment of Chicks" based on data from the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA), Institut de la Statistique du Québec (ISQ) and the Canadian Hatching Egg Producers (CHEP), as compiled by AAFC, AID, Poultry Section (Table S3).

The number of layers in each province in 2012 and rate-of-lay by province are based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada (Table S4). National egg production (1000 dozens) by province is based on StatsCan table 003-0020 (Production and disposition of eggs) (Table S5). The distribution of egg grades by province are taken from information collated by EFC from provincial egg boards, accessed via the Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada website. Egg grade size is taken from Schedule 3, Table 2 of the Egg Regulation.

Mass of eggs in each grade are defined in the Regulation as a minimum value, with increments of 7 g between sizes for Grade A eggs. Mass is not provided for other grades. Mass for each size of Grade A eggs (i.e. Jumbo, Extra Large, Large, Medium, Small, Peewee) was assumed to be the minimum size +3.5 g for each size (for PW, this was mass for Small minus 3.5 g). Based on national average grade distribution data from EFC, the mass of the other grades (i.e. B, C and NR) was calculated as the production weighted average of the national grade A size distribution. Egg production volume (in tonnes) was subsequently calculated by multiplying total production (in 1000 dozens) for each province in 2012 by the % size distribution and mass for each province (Table S6). Data for eggs broken for further processing by province were not available, hence breaking was assumed to occur in the province in which the eggs were produced.

Based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada, there were 19,981,902 layers housed in the five housing systems considered in this study in 2012 (note that this excludes 0.09% of Canadian layers which were group classified as "Aviary, Free Run, Free Range, Organic" rather than attributed to a specific housing system). Conventional cage systems accounted for the majority (92.3%) of layers. Survey data directly collected from laying facilities for this study represented 10.7% of the overall layer population. Propor- tional data representation was lowest for conventional cage hous- ing (9.3% of layers in conventional cages in Canada in 2012), and ranged from 20-40% for the other housing systems considered (Table S7). Given the much larger population of conventional cage layers and the large number of surveys collected from conventional cage facilities, this sample size is considered acceptable for the study. Similarly, the larger proportion of representation for the alternative housing systems is considered desirable in light of the much smaller number of layers housed in these systems in Canada (i.e. a greater proportional representation is necessary to achieve a similar degree of statistical power).

2.2.1.2. Modeling N, P and methane emissions from poultry manure. A nutrient mass balance model based on feed composition (prox- imate compositions of individual feed inputs were derived from www.feedipedia.org) and assuming that: (1) 2.2% of hen body mass is nitrogen and 0.6% is phosphorus and (2) eggs contain 1.7% ni- trogen and 0.21% phosphorus (following Koelsch (2007)) was used to calculate nitrogen and phosphorus excretion rates. In turn, estimated nitrogen excretion rates were used to calculate direct nitrous oxide, ammonia and nitric oxide emissions from manure management and indirect nitrous oxide emissions from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions following IPCC (2006) Tier 2 protocols and default emission factors at time of deposition, storage and application. These calculations took into account the propor- tion of each manure management system employed (i.e. weighted average emission factors are calculated). For losses of N due to ni- trate leaching during manure storage, IPCC suggests a range of 1-20%. The value employed in the current analysis is 20% for un- covered manure storage and 0% for covered manure storage. Following Dalgaard et al. (2008), phosphorus emissions were calculated based on an assumed 2.9% run-off rate at time of application of manure to agricultural lands. Methane emissions from manure management were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier I protocols, taking into account the proportions and resi- dency time of manure in dry and wet manure management sys- items. Methane emission factors for layers (0.03 kg and 1.2 kg CH4/ head/year for hens in dry and wet manure management systems, respectively) were applied for calculating emissions from breeder flock and pullet facilities.
2.2.1.3. Modeling feed input supply chains. Thirty distinct feed

inputs were reported as contributing to breeder flock, pullet and layer feeds. Of these, the three major inputs to feeds for egg pro- duction supply chains are corn, wheat and soybeans. The pro- portions of these inputs used in each province varies, as do the underpinning inputs of fertilizers, energy, pesticides, and other resources.

For the purpose of this study, Canadian-average models are developed for wheat, corn and soy, based on the following data sources and assumptions. National average fertilizer input rates are calculated by dividing total national nutrient inputs per crop in 2010-2011 as reported by the International Fertilizer Industry Association (Heffer, 2013; IFA, 2013) by total national production of each crop in 2011 as reported by StatsCan (Table 001-0010). Jayasundara et al. (2014) report detailed life cycle inventory models for corn production in Ontario. Since 70% of Canadian corn is pro- duced in Ontario, input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use are derived from this literature source.

The majority of wheat is produced in the prairie provinces (i.e. Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta). Input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use per tonne of wheat produced are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta, but are adjusted to reflect an assumed 3 rather than 4 tonne per hectare yield as per StatsCan for 2011. Similar data for soy production are derived from (S&T)2 (2010), which reports LCI data for the Canadian GHGenius model.

Other significant crop inputs to Canadian egg supply chain feeds include barley, peas and canola. For barley, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). Yields are adjusted to reflect the 2012 Canadian average as reported by StatsCan (2.9 tonnes/ha). For peas, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). The Canadian average yield based on StatsCan data from 2012 (2.3 tonnes/ha) is employed. Saskatchewan and Alberta account for 98% of Canadian pea production. For canola, input data per tonne are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta. Together, Alberta, Man- itoba and Saskatchewan account for 98% of Canadian canola pro- duction. Again, a Canadian average yield for 2012 based on StatsCan data is assumed (1.6 tonnes/ha).

Since organic egg production was among the housing systems modeled, it was also necessary to develop life cycle inventory data models for organic feed input production. Four significant raw materials were reported as inputs to organic feeds. These were organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. Pelletier et al. (2008) reported life cycle inventory data for major Canadian field crops, including organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. These life cycle inventory models were adopted for use in the current analysis.

Field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate and carbon dioxide (from urea fertilizers) emissions were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols using default emission fac- tors. A 2.9% surplus phosphate emission rate was assumed following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizers and pesticides were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, and all seed inputs 100 km by truck.

Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were defined based on data from the International Fertilizer Industry Association (IFA, 2013). Life cycle inventory data models for individual fertilizer in- puts were taken from the EcoInvent database. These models represent average production conditions in Europe.
Processing of wheat, soy, corn and canola was based on in- ventory data reported by Pelletier et al. (2009; 2010a,b; 2014a,b).

A variety of materials derived from rendered livestock products were reported as inputs to feeds utilized in the layer supply chain. These include both fat and meat/bone meal products from cattle, swine and poultry as well as poultry feather meal. The life cycle inventory models for beef and swine production employed in the current study were adopted from studies by Pelletier and col- leagues of contemporary production systems in the mid-western

United States (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with all US supply chain inputs replaced with equivalent Canadian inputs. Rendered poultry products were assumed to be derived from spent hens as modeled in the current analysis. Data for the production of ruminant and porcine meat and bone meal and fat follow Pelletier et al. (2014b, 2010a, 2010b, 2009). Other inputs to feed in the Canadian egg supply chain include sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids and other pre-mixes and supplements. Inventory data for these inputs were largely derived from the EcoInvent database. All mineral inputs were modeled as salt. Inventory data for the production of DL- methionine from Binder (2003) was used in proxy for all amino acid inputs. As pre-mixes and supplements may vary widely in composition and detailed composition data were not available, they were excluded from the analysis.

2.2.1.4. Compound feed production. Separate, consumption- weighted average feed formulations were modeled for each step of the egg supply chain. For pullets, one conventional and one organic feed formulation were modeled. For layers, a single, feed formulation was modeled for conventional layer feed. This feed was assumed to be used in conventional cage, enriched, free run and free range systems. A second organic layer feed formulation was modeled also, again based on consumption-weighted producer reported data.

2.2.1.5. Egg processing, packaging, and further processing. Survey data were obtained from 10 Canadian shell egg processing (i.e. washing, grading and packaging) facilities collectively repre- senting 157,024,543 dozen processed eggs in 2012.
Due to insufficient data regarding packaging of eggs and egg products, inventory data were adopted from Pelletier et al. (2013) for egg supply chains in the mid-western United States.

Survey data were obtained from only one egg breaking and further processing facility in Canada. For confidentiality reasons, it is not possible to report these data. For this reason, life cycle in- ventory data for egg breaking and production of pasteurized liquid egg were derived from Pelletier et al. (2013). These data were collected from six egg breaking and further processing facilities in the Midwestern United States in 2009, and represent 372,443,325 dozen eggs broken. Data are presented for pasteurized, liquid whole eggs only.

2.2.1.6. Energy carriers and mixes. Provincial electricity life cycle inventory models are taken from the EcoInvent database. Production-weighted average electricity mixes were modeled for each supply chain stage, including for crop input production, feed input processing, and breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, grading, and breaking facilities. The same primary energy carrier processes used in the EcoIn- vent Canadian provincial electricity mix processes are employed wherever otherwise required in the life cycle inventory models. This includes models for natural gas, liquid propane gas (lpg), diesel, fuel oil, and gasoline. The latter data are based on European models, and should be replaced with Canadian data once such data become available. For layer facilities, one production-weighted average housing energy mix was developed for conventional cage and enriched cage production, one for free run production, and one for free range and organic production.

2.2.1.7. Transportation. Production-weighted, producer-reported transport modes and distances were applied wherever possible. For all truck transport, the EcoInvent inventory model representing average European fleet conditions for trucks greater than 16 tonnes

was applied. This model is likely conservative in the Canadian context given the long-distance hauling characteristic of Canadian road freight. Similarly, all tractor transport was modeled using the average EcoInvent inventory model for a tractor and trailer.
