For layer facilities, one production-weighted average housing energy mix was developed for conventional cage and enriched cage production, one for free run production, and one for free range and organic production.

2.2.1.7. Transportation. Production-weighted, producer-reported transport modes and distances were applied wherever possible. For all truck transport, the EcoInvent inventory model representing average European fleet conditions for trucks greater than 16 tonnes

was applied. This model is likely conservative in the Canadian context given the long-distance hauling characteristic of Canadian road freight. Similarly, all tractor transport was modeled using the average EcoInvent inventory model for a tractor and trailer.
28: <header>2.2.1. Data sources and assumptions </header>

For the purpose of the current analysis, foreground system data are those directly collected from supply chain participants in the study. Foreground system data were collected from breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, shell egg processor, and breaker facilities via survey (see Supplemental Information). Efforts were made to ensure a representative sample by province, housing system type and farm size. In total, the 88 surveys collected from 104 layer fa- cilities represented roughly 10% of all laying hens nation-wide in 2012 (for sample sizes and representation for each housing system type, see Table S9).

Alberta Agriculture and Rural Development (AARD) previously commissioned the consulting firm, Quantis, to conduct an LCA study of egg production in Alberta (Courchesne et al., 2013). In order to minimize reporting burdens for Alberta egg farmers, the inventory data collected by Quantis and Egg Farmers of Alberta were adopted for use in the current analysis. For consistency with the life cycle inventory data collected in the current study for other provinces, a short-version survey was administered to those egg farmers that previously supplied data for the AARD study (see Supplementary Information) in order to collect the additional necessary data.

Surveys were administered by provincial egg boards and their field officers. Completed surveys were coded such that follow-up questions could be directed to data providers whilst ensuring data source confidentiality. All data were collected under guarantee of non-disclosure with respect to individual facilities. In the absence of adequate Canadian data, feed milling energy input data

were instead derived from recent LCA studies of the US egg in- dustry (Pelletier et al., 2014b, 2013). These data were collected from eleven reporting facilities in the United States in 2010 and repre- sent a total production of 2,679,405 tonnes of pullet and layer feed. Feed formulations were derived from directly collected, producer- reported survey data.

Certain practices may vary between facilities. For example, manure management strategies may entail liquid or solid manure handling and storage, variable storage times, and varied uses of the manure. In such cases, production-weighted average processes were defined for each housing system type based on directly collected data. In light of low data resolution/quality with respect to reported quantities of manure removed from facilities, a standard excretion rate was assumed for both pullet and layer facilities based on Pelletier et al. (2014b), and scaled relative to reported feed conversion efficiencies. For mass of manure handled (i.e. for the purpose of quantifying manure transport), the following assump- tions were also implemented:

<list>for liquid manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to the mass of manure excreted 
for dry manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to 50% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to uncovered storage, and 33% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to covered storage 
for free range and organic systems, it was assumed that 50% of manure was excreted outdoors and was not subject to handling</list>

Since egg supply chain activities are not evenly distributed be- tween provinces and each province has characteristic electricity grid mixes, national average mixes for each supply chain stage were developed based on Canadian statistical data for provincial egg sector activity volumes and Canadian provincial electricity mixes as available in the EcoInvent (2013) database.

Background system data for the current analysis include the production and supply of feed inputs, water, energy carriers, packaging and transport modes. Background system data other than feed input production and processing models were derived from the EcoInvent database, but modified where possible to best approximate Canadian conditions by using Canadian energy inputs/ mixes.

2.2.1.1. National and provincial layer supply chain statistics. For hatcheries, hatch rates were calculated for each province by dividing total eggs set in 2012 by chicks hatched. Eggs required per female chick set for pullet production was calculated using Agri- culture and Agri-Food Canada Poultry Section data tables for "eggs set for egg production," "chicks hatched for egg production," and "hatchery review: chicks destroyed" (Tables S1-S2).

For pullet facilities, total pullets placed for egg production is based on "Hatchery Review: Eggs Set and Chicks Hatched - Place- ment of Chicks" based on data from the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA), Institut de la Statistique du Québec (ISQ) and the Canadian Hatching Egg Producers (CHEP), as compiled by AAFC, AID, Poultry Section (Table S3).

The number of layers in each province in 2012 and rate-of-lay by province are based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada (Table S4). National egg production (1000 dozens) by province is based on StatsCan table 003-0020 (Production and disposition of eggs) (Table S5). The distribution of egg grades by province are taken from information collated by EFC from provincial egg boards, accessed via the Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada website. Egg grade size is taken from Schedule 3, Table 2 of the Egg Regulation.

Mass of eggs in each grade are defined in the Regulation as a minimum value, with increments of 7 g between sizes for Grade A eggs. Mass is not provided for other grades. Mass for each size of Grade A eggs (i.e. Jumbo, Extra Large, Large, Medium, Small, Peewee) was assumed to be the minimum size +3.5 g for each size (for PW, this was mass for Small minus 3.5 g). Based on national average grade distribution data from EFC, the mass of the other grades (i.e. B, C and NR) was calculated as the production weighted average of the national grade A size distribution. Egg production volume (in tonnes) was subsequently calculated by multiplying total production (in 1000 dozens) for each province in 2012 by the % size distribution and mass for each province (Table S6). Data for eggs broken for further processing by province were not available, hence breaking was assumed to occur in the province in which the eggs were produced.

Based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada, there were 19,981,902 layers housed in the five housing systems considered in this study in 2012 (note that this excludes 0.09% of Canadian layers which were group classified as "Aviary, Free Run, Free Range, Organic" rather than attributed to a specific housing system). Conventional cage systems accounted for the majority (92.3%) of layers. Survey data directly collected from laying facilities for this study represented 10.7% of the overall layer population. Propor- tional data representation was lowest for conventional cage hous- ing (9.3% of layers in conventional cages in Canada in 2012), and ranged from 20-40% for the other housing systems considered (Table S7). Given the much larger population of conventional cage layers and the large number of surveys collected from conventional cage facilities, this sample size is considered acceptable for the study. Similarly, the larger proportion of representation for the alternative housing systems is considered desirable in light of the much smaller number of layers housed in these systems in Canada (i.e. a greater proportional representation is necessary to achieve a similar degree of statistical power).

2.2.1.2. Modeling N, P and methane emissions from poultry manure. A nutrient mass balance model based on feed composition (prox- imate compositions of individual feed inputs were derived from www.feedipedia.org) and assuming that: (1) 2.2% of hen body mass is nitrogen and 0.6% is phosphorus and (2) eggs contain 1.7% ni- trogen and 0.21% phosphorus (following Koelsch (2007)) was used to calculate nitrogen and phosphorus excretion rates. In turn, estimated nitrogen excretion rates were used to calculate direct nitrous oxide, ammonia and nitric oxide emissions from manure management and indirect nitrous oxide emissions from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions following IPCC (2006) Tier 2 protocols and default emission factors at time of deposition, storage and application. These calculations took into account the propor- tion of each manure management system employed (i.e. weighted average emission factors are calculated). For losses of N due to ni- trate leaching during manure storage, IPCC suggests a range of 1-20%. The value employed in the current analysis is 20% for un- covered manure storage and 0% for covered manure storage. Following Dalgaard et al. (2008), phosphorus emissions were calculated based on an assumed 2.9% run-off rate at time of application of manure to agricultural lands. Methane emissions from manure management were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier I protocols, taking into account the proportions and resi- dency time of manure in dry and wet manure management sys- items. Methane emission factors for layers (0.03 kg and 1.2 kg CH4/ head/year for hens in dry and wet manure management systems, respectively) were applied for calculating emissions from breeder flock and pullet facilities.
2.2.1.3. Modeling feed input supply chains. Thirty distinct feed

inputs were reported as contributing to breeder flock, pullet and layer feeds. Of these, the three major inputs to feeds for egg pro- duction supply chains are corn, wheat and soybeans. The pro- portions of these inputs used in each province varies, as do the underpinning inputs of fertilizers, energy, pesticides, and other resources.

For the purpose of this study, Canadian-average models are developed for wheat, corn and soy, based on the following data sources and assumptions. National average fertilizer input rates are calculated by dividing total national nutrient inputs per crop in 2010-2011 as reported by the International Fertilizer Industry Association (Heffer, 2013; IFA, 2013) by total national production of each crop in 2011 as reported by StatsCan (Table 001-0010). Jayasundara et al. (2014) report detailed life cycle inventory models for corn production in Ontario. Since 70% of Canadian corn is pro- duced in Ontario, input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use are derived from this literature source.

The majority of wheat is produced in the prairie provinces (i.e. Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta). Input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use per tonne of wheat produced are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta, but are adjusted to reflect an assumed 3 rather than 4 tonne per hectare yield as per StatsCan for 2011. Similar data for soy production are derived from (S&T)2 (2010), which reports LCI data for the Canadian GHGenius model.

Other significant crop inputs to Canadian egg supply chain feeds include barley, peas and canola. For barley, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). Yields are adjusted to reflect the 2012 Canadian average as reported by StatsCan (2.9 tonnes/ha). For peas, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). The Canadian average yield based on StatsCan data from 2012 (2.3 tonnes/ha) is employed. Saskatchewan and Alberta account for 98% of Canadian pea production. For canola, input data per tonne are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta. Together, Alberta, Man- itoba and Saskatchewan account for 98% of Canadian canola pro- duction. Again, a Canadian average yield for 2012 based on StatsCan data is assumed (1.6 tonnes/ha).

Since organic egg production was among the housing systems modeled, it was also necessary to develop life cycle inventory data models for organic feed input production. Four significant raw materials were reported as inputs to organic feeds. These were organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. Pelletier et al. (2008) reported life cycle inventory data for major Canadian field crops, including organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. These life cycle inventory models were adopted for use in the current analysis.

Field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate and carbon dioxide (from urea fertilizers) emissions were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols using default emission fac- tors. A 2.9% surplus phosphate emission rate was assumed following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizers and pesticides were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, and all seed inputs 100 km by truck.

Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were defined based on data from the International Fertilizer Industry Association (IFA, 2013). Life cycle inventory data models for individual fertilizer in- puts were taken from the EcoInvent database. These models represent average production conditions in Europe.
Processing of wheat, soy, corn and canola was based on in- ventory data reported by Pelletier et al. (2009; 2010a,b; 2014a,b).

A variety of materials derived from rendered livestock products were reported as inputs to feeds utilized in the layer supply chain. These include both fat and meat/bone meal products from cattle, swine and poultry as well as poultry feather meal. The life cycle inventory models for beef and swine production employed in the current study were adopted from studies by Pelletier and col- leagues of contemporary production systems in the mid-western

United States (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with all US supply chain inputs replaced with equivalent Canadian inputs. Rendered poultry products were assumed to be derived from spent hens as modeled in the current analysis. Data for the production of ruminant and porcine meat and bone meal and fat follow Pelletier et al. (2014b, 2010a, 2010b, 2009). Other inputs to feed in the Canadian egg supply chain include sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids and other pre-mixes and supplements. Inventory data for these inputs were largely derived from the EcoInvent database. All mineral inputs were modeled as salt. Inventory data for the production of DL- methionine from Binder (2003) was used in proxy for all amino acid inputs. As pre-mixes and supplements may vary widely in composition and detailed composition data were not available, they were excluded from the analysis.

2.2.1.4. Compound feed production. Separate, consumption- weighted average feed formulations were modeled for each step of the egg supply chain. For pullets, one conventional and one organic feed formulation were modeled. For layers, a single, feed formulation was modeled for conventional layer feed. This feed was assumed to be used in conventional cage, enriched, free run and free range systems. A second organic layer feed formulation was modeled also, again based on consumption-weighted producer reported data.

2.2.1.5. Egg processing, packaging, and further processing. Survey data were obtained from 10 Canadian shell egg processing (i.e. washing, grading and packaging) facilities collectively repre- senting 157,024,543 dozen processed eggs in 2012.
Due to insufficient data regarding packaging of eggs and egg products, inventory data were adopted from Pelletier et al. (2013) for egg supply chains in the mid-western United States.

Survey data were obtained from only one egg breaking and further processing facility in Canada. For confidentiality reasons, it is not possible to report these data. For this reason, life cycle in- ventory data for egg breaking and production of pasteurized liquid egg were derived from Pelletier et al. (2013). These data were collected from six egg breaking and further processing facilities in the Midwestern United States in 2009, and represent 372,443,325 dozen eggs broken. Data are presented for pasteurized, liquid whole eggs only.

2.2.1.6. Energy carriers and mixes. Provincial electricity life cycle inventory models are taken from the EcoInvent database. Production-weighted average electricity mixes were modeled for each supply chain stage, including for crop input production, feed input processing, and breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, grading, and breaking facilities. The same primary energy carrier processes used in the EcoIn- vent Canadian provincial electricity mix processes are employed wherever otherwise required in the life cycle inventory models. This includes models for natural gas, liquid propane gas (lpg), diesel, fuel oil, and gasoline. The latter data are based on European models, and should be replaced with Canadian data once such data become available. For layer facilities, one production-weighted average housing energy mix was developed for conventional cage and enriched cage production, one for free run production, and one for free range and organic production.

2.2.1.7. Transportation. Production-weighted, producer-reported transport modes and distances were applied wherever possible. For all truck transport, the EcoInvent inventory model representing average European fleet conditions for trucks greater than 16 tonnes

was applied. This model is likely conservative in the Canadian context given the long-distance hauling characteristic of Canadian road freight. Similarly, all tractor transport was modeled using the average EcoInvent inventory model for a tractor and trailer.

were instead derived from recent LCA studies of the US egg in- dustry (Pelletier et al., 2014b, 2013). These data were collected from eleven reporting facilities in the United States in 2010 and repre- sent a total production of 2,679,405 tonnes of pullet and layer feed. Feed formulations were derived from directly collected, producer- reported survey data.

Certain practices may vary between facilities. For example, manure management strategies may entail liquid or solid manure handling and storage, variable storage times, and varied uses of the manure. In such cases, production-weighted average processes were defined for each housing system type based on directly collected data. In light of low data resolution/quality with respect to reported quantities of manure removed from facilities, a standard excretion rate was assumed for both pullet and layer facilities based on Pelletier et al. (2014b), and scaled relative to reported feed conversion efficiencies. For mass of manure handled (i.e. for the purpose of quantifying manure transport), the following assump- tions were also implemented:

<list>for liquid manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to the mass of manure excreted 
for dry manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to 50% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to uncovered storage, and 33% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to covered storage 
for free range and organic systems, it was assumed that 50% of manure was excreted outdoors and was not subject to handling</list>

Since egg supply chain activities are not evenly distributed be- tween provinces and each province has characteristic electricity grid mixes, national average mixes for each supply chain stage were developed based on Canadian statistical data for provincial egg sector activity volumes and Canadian provincial electricity mixes as available in the EcoInvent (2013) database.

Background system data for the current analysis include the production and supply of feed inputs, water, energy carriers, packaging and transport modes. Background system data other than feed input production and processing models were derived from the EcoInvent database, but modified where possible to best approximate Canadian conditions by using Canadian energy inputs/ mixes.

2.2.1.1. National and provincial layer supply chain statistics. For hatcheries, hatch rates were calculated for each province by dividing total eggs set in 2012 by chicks hatched. Eggs required per female chick set for pullet production was calculated using Agri- culture and Agri-Food Canada Poultry Section data tables for "eggs set for egg production," "chicks hatched for egg production," and "hatchery review: chicks destroyed" (Tables S1-S2).

For pullet facilities, total pullets placed for egg production is based on "Hatchery Review: Eggs Set and Chicks Hatched - Place- ment of Chicks" based on data from the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA), Institut de la Statistique du Québec (ISQ) and the Canadian Hatching Egg Producers (CHEP), as compiled by AAFC, AID, Poultry Section (Table S3).

The number of layers in each province in 2012 and rate-of-lay by province are based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada (Table S4). National egg production (1000 dozens) by province is based on StatsCan table 003-0020 (Production and disposition of eggs) (Table S5). The distribution of egg grades by province are taken from information collated by EFC from provincial egg boards, accessed via the Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada website. Egg grade size is taken from Schedule 3, Table 2 of the Egg Regulation.

Mass of eggs in each grade are defined in the Regulation as a minimum value, with increments of 7 g between sizes for Grade A eggs. Mass is not provided for other grades. Mass for each size of Grade A eggs (i.e. Jumbo, Extra Large, Large, Medium, Small, Peewee) was assumed to be the minimum size +3.5 g for each size (for PW, this was mass for Small minus 3.5 g). Based on national average grade distribution data from EFC, the mass of the other grades (i.e. B, C and NR) was calculated as the production weighted average of the national grade A size distribution. Egg production volume (in tonnes) was subsequently calculated by multiplying total production (in 1000 dozens) for each province in 2012 by the % size distribution and mass for each province (Table S6). Data for eggs broken for further processing by province were not available, hence breaking was assumed to occur in the province in which the eggs were produced.

Based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada, there were 19,981,902 layers housed in the five housing systems considered in this study in 2012 (note that this excludes 0.09% of Canadian layers which were group classified as "Aviary, Free Run, Free Range, Organic" rather than attributed to a specific housing system). Conventional cage systems accounted for the majority (92.3%) of layers. Survey data directly collected from laying facilities for this study represented 10.7% of the overall layer population. Propor- tional data representation was lowest for conventional cage hous- ing (9.3% of layers in conventional cages in Canada in 2012), and ranged from 20-40% for the other housing systems considered (Table S7). Given the much larger population of conventional cage layers and the large number of surveys collected from conventional cage facilities, this sample size is considered acceptable for the study. Similarly, the larger proportion of representation for the alternative housing systems is considered desirable in light of the much smaller number of layers housed in these systems in Canada (i.e. a greater proportional representation is necessary to achieve a similar degree of statistical power).

2.2.1.2. Modeling N, P and methane emissions from poultry manure. A nutrient mass balance model based on feed composition (prox- imate compositions of individual feed inputs were derived from www.feedipedia.org) and assuming that: (1) 2.2% of hen body mass is nitrogen and 0.6% is phosphorus and (2) eggs contain 1.7% ni- trogen and 0.21% phosphorus (following Koelsch (2007)) was used to calculate nitrogen and phosphorus excretion rates. In turn, estimated nitrogen excretion rates were used to calculate direct nitrous oxide, ammonia and nitric oxide emissions from manure management and indirect nitrous oxide emissions from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions following IPCC (2006) Tier 2 protocols and default emission factors at time of deposition, storage and application. These calculations took into account the propor- tion of each manure management system employed (i.e. weighted average emission factors are calculated). For losses of N due to ni- trate leaching during manure storage, IPCC suggests a range of 1-20%. The value employed in the current analysis is 20% for un- covered manure storage and 0% for covered manure storage. Following Dalgaard et al. (2008), phosphorus emissions were calculated based on an assumed 2.9% run-off rate at time of application of manure to agricultural lands. Methane emissions from manure management were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier I protocols, taking into account the proportions and resi- dency time of manure in dry and wet manure management sys- items. Methane emission factors for layers (0.03 kg and 1.2 kg CH4/ head/year for hens in dry and wet manure management systems, respectively) were applied for calculating emissions from breeder flock and pullet facilities.
2.2.1.3. Modeling feed input supply chains. Thirty distinct feed

inputs were reported as contributing to breeder flock, pullet and layer feeds. Of these, the three major inputs to feeds for egg pro- duction supply chains are corn, wheat and soybeans. The pro- portions of these inputs used in each province varies, as do the underpinning inputs of fertilizers, energy, pesticides, and other resources.

For the purpose of this study, Canadian-average models are developed for wheat, corn and soy, based on the following data sources and assumptions. National average fertilizer input rates are calculated by dividing total national nutrient inputs per crop in 2010-2011 as reported by the International Fertilizer Industry Association (Heffer, 2013; IFA, 2013) by total national production of each crop in 2011 as reported by StatsCan (Table 001-0010). Jayasundara et al. (2014) report detailed life cycle inventory models for corn production in Ontario. Since 70% of Canadian corn is pro- duced in Ontario, input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use are derived from this literature source.

The majority of wheat is produced in the prairie provinces (i.e. Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta). Input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use per tonne of wheat produced are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta, but are adjusted to reflect an assumed 3 rather than 4 tonne per hectare yield as per StatsCan for 2011. Similar data for soy production are derived from (S&T)2 (2010), which reports LCI data for the Canadian GHGenius model.

Other significant crop inputs to Canadian egg supply chain feeds include barley, peas and canola. For barley, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). Yields are adjusted to reflect the 2012 Canadian average as reported by StatsCan (2.9 tonnes/ha). For peas, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). The Canadian average yield based on StatsCan data from 2012 (2.3 tonnes/ha) is employed. Saskatchewan and Alberta account for 98% of Canadian pea production. For canola, input data per tonne are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta. Together, Alberta, Man- itoba and Saskatchewan account for 98% of Canadian canola pro- duction. Again, a Canadian average yield for 2012 based on StatsCan data is assumed (1.6 tonnes/ha).

Since organic egg production was among the housing systems modeled, it was also necessary to develop life cycle inventory data models for organic feed input production. Four significant raw materials were reported as inputs to organic feeds. These were organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. Pelletier et al. (2008) reported life cycle inventory data for major Canadian field crops, including organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. These life cycle inventory models were adopted for use in the current analysis.

Field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate and carbon dioxide (from urea fertilizers) emissions were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols using default emission fac- tors. A 2.9% surplus phosphate emission rate was assumed following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizers and pesticides were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, and all seed inputs 100 km by truck.

Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were defined based on data from the International Fertilizer Industry Association (IFA, 2013). Life cycle inventory data models for individual fertilizer in- puts were taken from the EcoInvent database. These models represent average production conditions in Europe.
Processing of wheat, soy, corn and canola was based on in- ventory data reported by Pelletier et al. (2009; 2010a,b; 2014a,b).

A variety of materials derived from rendered livestock products were reported as inputs to feeds utilized in the layer supply chain. These include both fat and meat/bone meal products from cattle, swine and poultry as well as poultry feather meal. The life cycle inventory models for beef and swine production employed in the current study were adopted from studies by Pelletier and col- leagues of contemporary production systems in the mid-western

United States (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with all US supply chain inputs replaced with equivalent Canadian inputs. Rendered poultry products were assumed to be derived from spent hens as modeled in the current analysis. Data for the production of ruminant and porcine meat and bone meal and fat follow Pelletier et al. (2014b, 2010a, 2010b, 2009). Other inputs to feed in the Canadian egg supply chain include sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids and other pre-mixes and supplements. Inventory data for these inputs were largely derived from the EcoInvent database. All mineral inputs were modeled as salt. Inventory data for the production of DL- methionine from Binder (2003) was used in proxy for all amino acid inputs. As pre-mixes and supplements may vary widely in composition and detailed composition data were not available, they were excluded from the analysis.

2.2.1.4. Compound feed production. Separate, consumption- weighted average feed formulations were modeled for each step of the egg supply chain. For pullets, one conventional and one organic feed formulation were modeled. For layers, a single, feed formulation was modeled for conventional layer feed. This feed was assumed to be used in conventional cage, enriched, free run and free range systems. A second organic layer feed formulation was modeled also, again based on consumption-weighted producer reported data.

2.2.1.5. Egg processing, packaging, and further processing. Survey data were obtained from 10 Canadian shell egg processing (i.e. washing, grading and packaging) facilities collectively repre- senting 157,024,543 dozen processed eggs in 2012.
Due to insufficient data regarding packaging of eggs and egg products, inventory data were adopted from Pelletier et al. (2013) for egg supply chains in the mid-western United States.

Survey data were obtained from only one egg breaking and further processing facility in Canada. For confidentiality reasons, it is not possible to report these data. For this reason, life cycle in- ventory data for egg breaking and production of pasteurized liquid egg were derived from Pelletier et al. (2013). These data were collected from six egg breaking and further processing facilities in the Midwestern United States in 2009, and represent 372,443,325 dozen eggs broken. Data are presented for pasteurized, liquid whole eggs only.

2.2.1.6. Energy carriers and mixes. Provincial electricity life cycle inventory models are taken from the EcoInvent database. Production-weighted average electricity mixes were modeled for each supply chain stage, including for crop input production, feed input processing, and breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, grading, and breaking facilities. The same primary energy carrier processes used in the EcoIn- vent Canadian provincial electricity mix processes are employed wherever otherwise required in the life cycle inventory models. This includes models for natural gas, liquid propane gas (lpg), diesel, fuel oil, and gasoline. The latter data are based on European models, and should be replaced with Canadian data once such data become available. For layer facilities, one production-weighted average housing energy mix was developed for conventional cage and enriched cage production, one for free run production, and one for free range and organic production.

2.2.1.7. Transportation. Production-weighted, producer-reported transport modes and distances were applied wherever possible. For all truck transport, the EcoInvent inventory model representing average European fleet conditions for trucks greater than 16 tonnes

was applied. This model is likely conservative in the Canadian context given the long-distance hauling characteristic of Canadian road freight. Similarly, all tractor transport was modeled using the average EcoInvent inventory model for a tractor and trailer.

Background system data for the current analysis include the production and supply of feed inputs, water, energy carriers, packaging and transport modes. Background system data other than feed input production and processing models were derived from the EcoInvent database, but modified where possible to best approximate Canadian conditions by using Canadian energy inputs/ mixes.

2.2.1.1. National and provincial layer supply chain statistics. For hatcheries, hatch rates were calculated for each province by dividing total eggs set in 2012 by chicks hatched. Eggs required per female chick set for pullet production was calculated using Agri- culture and Agri-Food Canada Poultry Section data tables for "eggs set for egg production," "chicks hatched for egg production," and "hatchery review: chicks destroyed" (Tables S1-S2).

For pullet facilities, total pullets placed for egg production is based on "Hatchery Review: Eggs Set and Chicks Hatched - Place- ment of Chicks" based on data from the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA), Institut de la Statistique du Québec (ISQ) and the Canadian Hatching Egg Producers (CHEP), as compiled by AAFC, AID, Poultry Section (Table S3).

The number of layers in each province in 2012 and rate-of-lay by province are based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada (Table S4). National egg production (1000 dozens) by province is based on StatsCan table 003-0020 (Production and disposition of eggs) (Table S5). The distribution of egg grades by province are taken from information collated by EFC from provincial egg boards, accessed via the Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada website. Egg grade size is taken from Schedule 3, Table 2 of the Egg Regulation.

Mass of eggs in each grade are defined in the Regulation as a minimum value, with increments of 7 g between sizes for Grade A eggs. Mass is not provided for other grades. Mass for each size of Grade A eggs (i.e. Jumbo, Extra Large, Large, Medium, Small, Peewee) was assumed to be the minimum size +3.5 g for each size (for PW, this was mass for Small minus 3.5 g). Based on national average grade distribution data from EFC, the mass of the other grades (i.e. B, C and NR) was calculated as the production weighted average of the national grade A size distribution. Egg production volume (in tonnes) was subsequently calculated by multiplying total production (in 1000 dozens) for each province in 2012 by the % size distribution and mass for each province (Table S6). Data for eggs broken for further processing by province were not available, hence breaking was assumed to occur in the province in which the eggs were produced.

Based on data compiled by Egg Farmers of Canada, there were 19,981,902 layers housed in the five housing systems considered in this study in 2012 (note that this excludes 0.09% of Canadian layers which were group classified as "Aviary, Free Run, Free Range, Organic" rather than attributed to a specific housing system). Conventional cage systems accounted for the majority (92.3%) of layers. Survey data directly collected from laying facilities for this study represented 10.7% of the overall layer population. Propor- tional data representation was lowest for conventional cage hous- ing (9.3% of layers in conventional cages in Canada in 2012), and ranged from 20-40% for the other housing systems considered (Table S7). Given the much larger population of conventional cage layers and the large number of surveys collected from conventional cage facilities, this sample size is considered acceptable for the study. Similarly, the larger proportion of representation for the alternative housing systems is considered desirable in light of the much smaller number of layers housed in these systems in Canada (i.e. a greater proportional representation is necessary to achieve a similar degree of statistical power).

2.2.1.2. Modeling N, P and methane emissions from poultry manure. A nutrient mass balance model based on feed composition (prox- imate compositions of individual feed inputs were derived from www.feedipedia.org) and assuming that: (1) 2.2% of hen body mass is nitrogen and 0.6% is phosphorus and (2) eggs contain 1.7% ni- trogen and 0.21% phosphorus (following Koelsch (2007)) was used to calculate nitrogen and phosphorus excretion rates. In turn, estimated nitrogen excretion rates were used to calculate direct nitrous oxide, ammonia and nitric oxide emissions from manure management and indirect nitrous oxide emissions from nitrate leaching and ammonia emissions following IPCC (2006) Tier 2 protocols and default emission factors at time of deposition, storage and application. These calculations took into account the propor- tion of each manure management system employed (i.e. weighted average emission factors are calculated). For losses of N due to ni- trate leaching during manure storage, IPCC suggests a range of 1-20%. The value employed in the current analysis is 20% for un- covered manure storage and 0% for covered manure storage. Following Dalgaard et al. (2008), phosphorus emissions were calculated based on an assumed 2.9% run-off rate at time of application of manure to agricultural lands. Methane emissions from manure management were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier I protocols, taking into account the proportions and resi- dency time of manure in dry and wet manure management sys- items. Methane emission factors for layers (0.03 kg and 1.2 kg CH4/ head/year for hens in dry and wet manure management systems, respectively) were applied for calculating emissions from breeder flock and pullet facilities.
2.2.1.3. Modeling feed input supply chains. Thirty distinct feed

inputs were reported as contributing to breeder flock, pullet and layer feeds. Of these, the three major inputs to feeds for egg pro- duction supply chains are corn, wheat and soybeans. The pro- portions of these inputs used in each province varies, as do the underpinning inputs of fertilizers, energy, pesticides, and other resources.

For the purpose of this study, Canadian-average models are developed for wheat, corn and soy, based on the following data sources and assumptions. National average fertilizer input rates are calculated by dividing total national nutrient inputs per crop in 2010-2011 as reported by the International Fertilizer Industry Association (Heffer, 2013; IFA, 2013) by total national production of each crop in 2011 as reported by StatsCan (Table 001-0010). Jayasundara et al. (2014) report detailed life cycle inventory models for corn production in Ontario. Since 70% of Canadian corn is pro- duced in Ontario, input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use are derived from this literature source.

The majority of wheat is produced in the prairie provinces (i.e. Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta). Input data for pesticides, seeds and energy use per tonne of wheat produced are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta, but are adjusted to reflect an assumed 3 rather than 4 tonne per hectare yield as per StatsCan for 2011. Similar data for soy production are derived from (S&T)2 (2010), which reports LCI data for the Canadian GHGenius model.

Other significant crop inputs to Canadian egg supply chain feeds include barley, peas and canola. For barley, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). Yields are adjusted to reflect the 2012 Canadian average as reported by StatsCan (2.9 tonnes/ha). For peas, input data per tonne are taken from (S&T)2 (2008). The Canadian average yield based on StatsCan data from 2012 (2.3 tonnes/ha) is employed. Saskatchewan and Alberta account for 98% of Canadian pea production. For canola, input data per tonne are taken from ChemInfo and (S&T)2 (2013) for Alberta. Together, Alberta, Man- itoba and Saskatchewan account for 98% of Canadian canola pro- duction. Again, a Canadian average yield for 2012 based on StatsCan data is assumed (1.6 tonnes/ha).

Since organic egg production was among the housing systems modeled, it was also necessary to develop life cycle inventory data models for organic feed input production. Four significant raw materials were reported as inputs to organic feeds. These were organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. Pelletier et al. (2008) reported life cycle inventory data for major Canadian field crops, including organic corn, wheat, soy and canola. These life cycle inventory models were adopted for use in the current analysis.

Field-level ammonia, nitrous oxide, nitric oxide, nitrate and carbon dioxide (from urea fertilizers) emissions were calculated following IPCC (2006) Tier 1 protocols using default emission fac- tors. A 2.9% surplus phosphate emission rate was assumed following Dalgaard et al. (2008). All fertilizers and pesticides were assumed to be transported 1000 km by truck, and all seed inputs 100 km by truck.

Canadian national average fertilizer mixes were defined based on data from the International Fertilizer Industry Association (IFA, 2013). Life cycle inventory data models for individual fertilizer in- puts were taken from the EcoInvent database. These models represent average production conditions in Europe.
Processing of wheat, soy, corn and canola was based on in- ventory data reported by Pelletier et al. (2009; 2010a,b; 2014a,b).

A variety of materials derived from rendered livestock products were reported as inputs to feeds utilized in the layer supply chain. These include both fat and meat/bone meal products from cattle, swine and poultry as well as poultry feather meal. The life cycle inventory models for beef and swine production employed in the current study were adopted from studies by Pelletier and col- leagues of contemporary production systems in the mid-western

United States (Pelletier et al., 2010a, 2010b), with all US supply chain inputs replaced with equivalent Canadian inputs. Rendered poultry products were assumed to be derived from spent hens as modeled in the current analysis. Data for the production of ruminant and porcine meat and bone meal and fat follow Pelletier et al. (2014b, 2010a, 2010b, 2009). Other inputs to feed in the Canadian egg supply chain include sources of calcium, phosphate, salt, minerals, amino acids and other pre-mixes and supplements. Inventory data for these inputs were largely derived from the EcoInvent database. All mineral inputs were modeled as salt. Inventory data for the production of DL- methionine from Binder (2003) was used in proxy for all amino acid inputs. As pre-mixes and supplements may vary widely in composition and detailed composition data were not available, they were excluded from the analysis.

2.2.1.4. Compound feed production. Separate, consumption- weighted average feed formulations were modeled for each step of the egg supply chain. For pullets, one conventional and one organic feed formulation were modeled. For layers, a single, feed formulation was modeled for conventional layer feed. This feed was assumed to be used in conventional cage, enriched, free run and free range systems. A second organic layer feed formulation was modeled also, again based on consumption-weighted producer reported data.

2.2.1.5. Egg processing, packaging, and further processing. Survey data were obtained from 10 Canadian shell egg processing (i.e. washing, grading and packaging) facilities collectively repre- senting 157,024,543 dozen processed eggs in 2012.
Due to insufficient data regarding packaging of eggs and egg products, inventory data were adopted from Pelletier et al. (2013) for egg supply chains in the mid-western United States.

Survey data were obtained from only one egg breaking and further processing facility in Canada. For confidentiality reasons, it is not possible to report these data. For this reason, life cycle in- ventory data for egg breaking and production of pasteurized liquid egg were derived from Pelletier et al. (2013). These data were collected from six egg breaking and further processing facilities in the Midwestern United States in 2009, and represent 372,443,325 dozen eggs broken. Data are presented for pasteurized, liquid whole eggs only.

2.2.1.6. Energy carriers and mixes. Provincial electricity life cycle inventory models are taken from the EcoInvent database. Production-weighted average electricity mixes were modeled for each supply chain stage, including for crop input production, feed input processing, and breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, grading, and breaking facilities. The same primary energy carrier processes used in the EcoIn- vent Canadian provincial electricity mix processes are employed wherever otherwise required in the life cycle inventory models. This includes models for natural gas, liquid propane gas (lpg), diesel, fuel oil, and gasoline. The latter data are based on European models, and should be replaced with Canadian data once such data become available. For layer facilities, one production-weighted average housing energy mix was developed for conventional cage and enriched cage production, one for free run production, and one for free range and organic production.

2.2.1.7. Transportation. Production-weighted, producer-reported transport modes and distances were applied wherever possible. For all truck transport, the EcoInvent inventory model representing average European fleet conditions for trucks greater than 16 tonnes

was applied. This model is likely conservative in the Canadian context given the long-distance hauling characteristic of Canadian road freight. Similarly, all tractor transport was modeled using the average EcoInvent inventory model for a tractor and trailer.
33: <header>2.2.1. Data sources and assumptions </header>

For the purpose of the current analysis, foreground system data are those directly collected from supply chain participants in the study. Foreground system data were collected from breeder flock, hatchery, pullet, layer, shell egg processor, and breaker facilities via survey (see Supplemental Information). Efforts were made to ensure a representative sample by province, housing system type and farm size. In total, the 88 surveys collected from 104 layer fa- cilities represented roughly 10% of all laying hens nation-wide in 2012 (for sample sizes and representation for each housing system type, see Table S9).

Alberta Agriculture and Rural Development (AARD) previously commissioned the consulting firm, Quantis, to conduct an LCA study of egg production in Alberta (Courchesne et al., 2013). In order to minimize reporting burdens for Alberta egg farmers, the inventory data collected by Quantis and Egg Farmers of Alberta were adopted for use in the current analysis. For consistency with the life cycle inventory data collected in the current study for other provinces, a short-version survey was administered to those egg farmers that previously supplied data for the AARD study (see Supplementary Information) in order to collect the additional necessary data.

Surveys were administered by provincial egg boards and their field officers. Completed surveys were coded such that follow-up questions could be directed to data providers whilst ensuring data source confidentiality. All data were collected under guarantee of non-disclosure with respect to individual facilities. In the absence of adequate Canadian data, feed milling energy input data

were instead derived from recent LCA studies of the US egg in- dustry (Pelletier et al., 2014b, 2013). These data were collected from eleven reporting facilities in the United States in 2010 and repre- sent a total production of 2,679,405 tonnes of pullet and layer feed. Feed formulations were derived from directly collected, producer- reported survey data.

Certain practices may vary between facilities. For example, manure management strategies may entail liquid or solid manure handling and storage, variable storage times, and varied uses of the manure. In such cases, production-weighted average processes were defined for each housing system type based on directly collected data. In light of low data resolution/quality with respect to reported quantities of manure removed from facilities, a standard excretion rate was assumed for both pullet and layer facilities based on Pelletier et al. (2014b), and scaled relative to reported feed conversion efficiencies. For mass of manure handled (i.e. for the purpose of quantifying manure transport), the following assump- tions were also implemented:

<list>for liquid manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to the mass of manure excreted 
for dry manure management systems, mass of manure handled was assumed equivalent to 50% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to uncovered storage, and 33% of the mass of manure excreted for stockpiled manure subject to covered storage 
for free range and organic systems, it was assumed that 50% of manure was excreted outdoors and was not subject to handling</list>

Since egg supply chain activities are not evenly distributed be- tween provinces and each province has characteristic electricity grid mixes, national average mixes for each supply chain stage were developed based on Canadian statistical data for provincial egg sector activity volumes and Canadian provincial electricity mixes as available in the EcoInvent (2013) database.

34: ISO-14044 requires a data quality assessment for studies that provide comparative assertions to be disclosed to the public. The intention of such assessments is to make transparent any potential weaknesses in the data that support the analysis in order to ensure that low quality data do not unduly influence the study outcomes and communicated results. Data quality assessments also provide the necessary basis for making iterative improvements to data quality, as well as signaling potentially important variables for sensitivity analyses. Table S1 describes the data quality criteria and scoring levels that were used to assess the data sourced for the current analysis. Table S2 describes key data and the data quality scores that were assigned for each. The data scores are reported on a numeric scale from 1 (highest quality) to 4 (lowest quality) and are also color coded (green = high quality, yellow = acceptable quality, orange = low quality, red - unacceptable quality) for ease of interpretation. It should be noted that such data quality assess- ments are, to some extent, subjective. Data indicated to be of high importance but low or unacceptable quality would be prioritized for data improvement activities and sensitivity analyses. However, the overall quality of the life cycle inventory data set for the current analysis was quite high, with low quality data scores only assigned to a limited number of data points of medium or low importance.
35: Three important types of uncertainty may be encountered in life cycle assessment studies. These are:

<list>Inventory data uncertainty 
Characterization model uncertainty 
LCI model uncertainty (for example errors in setting system boundaries, cut-off criteria, etc.)</list>

Limited guidance is available for data uncertainty management in LCA studies. Monte Carlo simulations are sometimes used to assess inventory data uncertainty, often based on assumed rather than actual data distributions. Since uncertainty is additive, and propagates through LCI models and LCIA results, limited accounting and reporting of uncertainty associated with LCA results may be very misleading. Moreover, very few published studies account for characterization model uncertainty, and none account for LCI model uncertainty. Given the current unavailability of standardized uncertainty assessment protocols for LCA, uncertainty was not assessed in the current study. However, data ranges are reported for all directly collected data.
36: Cumulative energy use, land use, direct water withdrawal, and GHG, acidifying and eutrophying emissions were evaluated. Energy use (MJ) was quantified following the Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) method (Frischknecht et al., 2003), which accounts for con- version efficiencies and the quality of energy inputs. Land use was quantified by summing the direct land use requirements related to poultry feed and other input supply chains. Water use was similarly quantified based on reported data from egg supply chain facilities as well as the water inputs to other background activities such as fertilizer production. It should be noted that both land and water use are inventory indicators only (i.e. as opposed to an impact potential indicator), and quantify all direct land occupation and

water withdrawals in the life cycle on an equivalent basis (i.e. characterization factors are not applied to weight different uses or sources). Salt water withdrawals were not included in the in- ventory, rather only fresh water withdrawals were considered. Water flowing through turbines for electricity generation was also excluded from the analysis. Global warming (CO2-equivalency over a 100-year time horizon according to IPCC, 2006), acidification (SO2-equivalency), and eutrophication (PO4-equivalency) poten- tials were quantified according to the CML 2 Baseline 2000 method (Guinee et al., 2001).

These assessment methods (other than the water and land use inventory indicators) follow the problem-oriented mid-point approach, meaning that results are expressed in terms of their potential resource and environmental impacts (as measured in resources used or emissions to the environment) rather than actual damage levels.

These assessment methods (other than the water and land use inventory indicators) follow the problem-oriented mid-point approach, meaning that results are expressed in terms of their potential resource and environmental impacts (as measured in resources used or emissions to the environment) rather than actual damage levels.
38: <header>2.3.1. Scenario modeling potential impact mitigation strategies </header>

Three mitigation scenarios were evaluated to assess the extent to which changes in inputs to and layer performance in conven- tional cage layer facilities in Canada might serve to mitigate current levels of resource use and emissions associated with egg

production. The scenarios were defined in light of insights provided by the life cycle inventory analysis as well the life cycle impact assessment results for Canadian layer systems with respect to key drivers of resource use and emissions (Table S34, Fig. 11).

Since feed conversion ratios and rate-of-lay are among the central determining factors for the overall cradle-to-facility gate resource use and emissions association with egg production, Sce- nario 1 evaluates the extent to which these can be reduced by achieving best-reported feed conversion efficiencies and highest rate-of-lay in conventional cage layer systems. Scenario 2 evaluates the efficacy of least-environmental cost feed sourcing. Here, it is assumed that organic layer feeds are used in the Canadian average conventional cage layer system, with all other performance vari- ables (other than changes in emissions related to different N and P excretion rates) held constant. Scenario 3 evaluates the effect of combining these improvement variables (i.e. best reported FCR and rate-of-lay for conventional cage systems along with the use of organic layer feeds).

46:
<list>3.1. Life cycle inventory results 
With the exception of LCI data for the major hen housing sys- tems (Table 1) detailed tables reporting life cycle inventory data for each supply chain activity as modelled in the current analysis are provided in the Supplementary Information file, as described below. This includes information regarding the sample size for each data set, as well as high and low reported values. 
3.1.1. Agricultural production for feed inputs 
All life cycle inventory data models for the production of agri- cultural feed inputs were structured in a methodologically consis- tent manner. Table S8 describes in detail the input and output data per tonne of crop that were used for each conventional feed crop production inventory model. Table S9 similarly describes the data employed for each of the organic feed crop production inventory models. 
3.1.2. Directly collected and proxy data for layer egg supply chain activities 
For LCI data for feeding milling and feed composition (specific to breeder flocks, and both conventional and organic pullets and layers), see Tables S10-S13. 
Tables S14-S24 report the life cycle inventory data that were either directly collected or adapted from methodologically consis- tent studies of US egg supply chains in order to characterize the activities associated with breeder flocks, hatcheries, pullet pro- duction, layer facilities (detailed per housing system type), egg processing, and egg breaking/further processing activities in Can- ada in 2012. All data are reported as production-weighted averages,</list>

and both low and high reported values for each data point are also provided. Sample size information is also described. Considerable variability is evident with respect to many data points. It is un- certain where and to what extent this variability characterizes actual differences in operational reality versus data reporting error. Obvious outliers have been excluded from the reported data. Layer facilities

48: Table 2, Tables S25-S29 and Figs. 2-6 report the life cycle impact assessment results for each stage of the national average Canadian egg supply chain considered, up to the pullet facility gate. Fig. 7, Figs. S1-S5, and Tables S26-S30 report the life cycle impact assessment results by layer housing system type. Tables S35-S36 and Figs. 8-9 report the life cycle impact assessment results for shell egg processing and egg breaking and further processing fa- cilities. Fig. 10 and Table S37 present the marginal life cycle impacts of transporting eggs to retail outlets, assuming transportation dis- tances of 100, 500 or 1000 km. Fig. 11 and Table S38 report the results of three scenario analyses which evaluate: (1) the mitigation potential of achieving best reported performance benchmarks with respect to feed conversion efficiency and rate-of-lay; (2), least environmental cost feed sourcing, and; (3) the combination of these two variables.

Resource use and emissions vary widely between the inputs sourced for feeds used in the Canadian egg supply chain (Table 2). For example, greenhouse gas emissions per tonne of feed input range from as low as 249 kg CO2-e per tonne for organic soy meal to as high as 58.2 tonnes CO2-e/tonne for ruminant tallow. In general, the amount of resources required and emissions produced are considerably higher for materials derived from livestock processing co-products compared to those derived from agricultural crops. This finding is intuitive in light of feed conversion efficiencies and

Life cycle impact assessment results (kg of acidifying, eutrophying or greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, m² of land use, m³ of water use and MJ of energy use) at the feedmill gate per tonne of feed input sourced for egg supply chain feeds in Canada in 2012.
Fig. 2. Comparative life cycle impacts for egg supply chain feeds produced in Canada in 2012 (impacts for each feed and category scaled relative to highest impact feed).
Fig. 3. Contributions of inputs to egg production from breeder flock faclities to cradle- to-facility gate life cycle acidifying, eutrophying and GHG emssions, and land, water, and energy use.
Fig. 4. Contributions of inputs to female chick production in hatchery faclities to cradle-to-facility gate life cycle acidifying, eutrophying and GHG emssions, and land, water, and energy use.

associated resource use and emissions for livestock production. For example, a feed input derived from processing co-products from an animal that has been raised on grain products will obviously have much higher resource use and emissions relative to those associ- ated with the grain products alone. Here, the impacts attributable to the animal product reflect the resource use and emissions associated with producing the materials that the animal was fed, and other inputs to animal production such as direct energy inputs,
Fig. 5. Contributions of inputs to pullet production in pullet faclities to cradle-to- facility gate life cycle acidifying, eutrophying and GHG emssions, and land, water, and energy use.

Fig. 6. Contributions of inputs to organic pullet production in organic pullet faclities to cradle-to-facility gate life cycle acidifying, eutrophying and GHG emssions, and land, water, and energy use.
Fig. 7. Life cycle impact assessment results for Canadian national average egg pro- duction compared to impacts by housing system (all impacts scaled relative to the national average results).

manure management, transportation, slaughter and rendering (which typically produces products with much lower moisture content than the raw material, effectively multiplying upstream resource use and emissions).
Among products of animal origin, those derived from cattle processing co-products are particularly impactful. Again, this largely reflects the much lower feed conversion efficiency of cattle
Fig. 8. Contributions of inputs to shell egg processing and packaging to cradle-to- facility gate life cycle acidifying, eutrophying and GHG emssions, and land, water, and energy use.
Fig. 9. Contributions of inputs to packaged, pasteurized liquid whole eggs to cradle-to- facility gate life cycle acidifying, eutrophying and GHG emssions, and land, water, and energy use.
Fig. 10. Comparative cradle-to-customer life cycle resource use and emissions for packaged shell eggs under different truck transport distance scenarios.

compared to swine or poultry, along with physiological and hus- bandry considerations particular to cattle (for example, enteric methane production, the maintenance of cow-calf herds with low fecundity, and cultivation and/or maintenance of pasture for forage).
However, resource use and emissions are also quite variable between feed inputs derived from agricultural crops. Leguminous crops like soy and peas, which fix their own nitrogen, tend to have
Fig. 11. Comparative cradle-to-layer facility gate life cycle impacts of eggs produced in conventional cage systems in Canada in 2012 compared to three mitigation scenarios.

lower impacts than non-leguminous crops (the production and use of nitrogen fertilizers is a key driver of resource use and emissions for crop production). Similarly, organic crops tend to be less resource (other than land) and emissions intensive than equivalent, conventionally produced crops, largely due to differences in ni- trogen procurement strategies. For these reasons, organic layer and pullet feeds (which don't include materials derived from livestock) have considerably lower resource demands and emissions compared to conventional feeds. Of the five feed formulations modeled, conventional pullet feeds were most impactful due to the amount and kinds of animal- derived inputs utilized in the feed (Fig. 2, Table S25).

57: Acidifying and eutrophying emissions associated with breeder flock facilities are primarily attributable to manure management (roughly 66%), followed by feed inputs (roughly 33%). In contrast, feed inputs are the largest contributor to supply chain GHG emis- sions (roughly 66%) followed by manure management (roughly 20%). Resource use is predominantly attributable to feed inputs. Direct water use in breeder flock facilities makes a modest contri- bution to supply chain water use, and direct energy inputs make small contributions (i.e. less than 10%) to overall cradle-to-facility gate supply chain water use and energy use associated with eggs produced in breeder flock facilities (Fig. 3, Table S26).
58: For hatcheries, upstream activities related to the production of eggs in breeder flock facilities account for the majority of supply chain emissions. Only for GHG emissions do actual hatchery-level activities, in the form of direct energy inputs, make a non-trivial (i.e. 29%) contribution. Egg inputs to hatcheries are similarly the most important contributor to resource use, accounting for almost 100% of land use, 45% of water use, and 54% of energy use (Fig. 4, Table S27).
59: The distribution of life cycle resource use and emissions for conventional pullet production show a similar overall pattern to that of breeder flock facilities. Manure management contributes a large share of acidifying (52%) and eutrophying (44%) emissions.
60: Life cycle impact assessment results (kg of acidifying, eutrophying and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, m² of land use, m³ of water use and MJ of energy use) per tonne of eggs produced in Canada in 2012 by housing system as well as national average production.

|Acidifying Emissions kg SO2- eq|Eutrophying Emissions kg PO4-eq|GHG Emissions kg CO2-eq|Land Use m²|Water Use m³|Energy Use MJ National Average|78.0|24.3|2419.6|7517.9|16.5|11222.6 Conventional Cage|78.4|24.4|2442.5|7545.0|16.5|11247.7 Enriched Cage|82.5|26.8|2306.2|8122.8|16.6|12068.1 Free Run|80.6|25.6|2403.4|7896.6|18.5|11811.2 Free Range|69.8|23.8|2398.2|7975.1|17.8|11470.6 Organic|46.6|14.8|1368.5|4573.2|13.2|7951.0

However, feed inputs once again account for the largest share of supply chain GHG emissions (68%), land use (98%), water use (56%) and energy use (78%). Direct energy inputs to pullet facilities make modest contributions to GHG emissions (12%), water use (18%) and energy use (17%). Direct water inputs account for 20% of supply chain water use (Fig. 5, Table S28).

For organic pullet production, feed inputs make a much smaller proportional contribution to resource use and emissions, due to the lower impacts associated with the agricultural materials used in organic feeds. Here, manure management is responsible for the majority of acidifying (63%) and eutrophying (64%) emissions, while direct energy inputs are the largest contributor to GHG emissions (50%) and energy use (79%). Feed inputs remain the largest contributor to land use (96%) and water use (39%). Direct water use accounts for 27% of overall, supply chain water use. The share of resource use and emissions associated with chicks sourced from hatcheries is small (Fig. 6, Table S29).

3.2.5. Layer facilities - comparisons by housing system type Table 3 and Fig. 7 describe the life cycle resource use and emissions associated with egg production in Canada in 2012 by housing system type, as well as the national production-weighted average. In general, the differences between housing systems is very small other than for organic production, where resource use and emissions are substantially lower per tonne of eggs produced. The lower estimated impacts for organic production is primarily due to differences in impacts related to feed production rather than to egg-farm level differences. Indeed, reported feed conversion efficiency is slightly lower for organic compared to conventional production, and reported mortality rates are roughly twice as high. For the free range and organic systems, acidifying and eutro- phying emissions are slightly lower than the national average due to the share of manure deposited outdoors, which reduces manure handling-related emissions by a small amount. Resource use for free range production is, however, slightly higher due to the lower reported feed conversion efficiency for free range systems (i.e. 2.17 kg of feed/kg of eggs compared to 2.00 for conventional cage production).

Tables S31-S34 and Figs. S1-S5 report the life cycle impact assessment results by layer housing system type. In general, the distribution of resource use and emissions associated with egg production in layer facilities is quite similar for conventional cage, enriched cage, free run and free range production. Pullets account for roughly 20% of resource use and emissions across these four housing systems. Manure management and feed inputs are the largest contributors to acidifying and eutrophying emissions, while feed inputs dominate supply chain GHG emissions, land, water and energy use. Direct water use accounts for roughly 15-20% of overall cradle-to-layer facility gate water use. For organic egg production, feed-related impacts are proportionately less important, although they remain the dominant contributor to land, water and energy use.

Direct water inputs account for 20% of supply chain water use (Fig. 5, Table S28).

For organic pullet production, feed inputs make a much smaller proportional contribution to resource use and emissions, due to the lower impacts associated with the agricultural materials used in organic feeds. Here, manure management is responsible for the majority of acidifying (63%) and eutrophying (64%) emissions, while direct energy inputs are the largest contributor to GHG emissions (50%) and energy use (79%). Feed inputs remain the largest contributor to land use (96%) and water use (39%). Direct water use accounts for 27% of overall, supply chain water use. The share of resource use and emissions associated with chicks sourced from hatcheries is small (Fig. 6, Table S29).

3.2.5. Layer facilities - comparisons by housing system type Table 3 and Fig. 7 describe the life cycle resource use and emissions associated with egg production in Canada in 2012 by housing system type, as well as the national production-weighted average. In general, the differences between housing systems is very small other than for organic production, where resource use and emissions are substantially lower per tonne of eggs produced. The lower estimated impacts for organic production is primarily due to differences in impacts related to feed production rather than to egg-farm level differences. Indeed, reported feed conversion efficiency is slightly lower for organic compared to conventional production, and reported mortality rates are roughly twice as high. For the free range and organic systems, acidifying and eutro- phying emissions are slightly lower than the national average due to the share of manure deposited outdoors, which reduces manure handling-related emissions by a small amount. Resource use for free range production is, however, slightly higher due to the lower reported feed conversion efficiency for free range systems (i.e. 2.17 kg of feed/kg of eggs compared to 2.00 for conventional cage production).

Tables S31-S34 and Figs. S1-S5 report the life cycle impact assessment results by layer housing system type. In general, the distribution of resource use and emissions associated with egg production in layer facilities is quite similar for conventional cage, enriched cage, free run and free range production. Pullets account for roughly 20% of resource use and emissions across these four housing systems. Manure management and feed inputs are the largest contributors to acidifying and eutrophying emissions, while feed inputs dominate supply chain GHG emissions, land, water and energy use. Direct water use accounts for roughly 15-20% of overall cradle-to-layer facility gate water use. For organic egg production, feed-related impacts are proportionately less important, although they remain the dominant contributor to land, water and energy use.
