64: Eggs from layer facilities contribute the majority of resource use and emissions associated with processed, packaged eggs. Direct water use in egg grading and packaging facilities contributes a minor share (4%) of supply chain water use. The production and transportation of packaging contributes small shares of GHG emissions (4%) and land use (2%) and modest shares of water (12%) and energy use 16%) (Table S35, Fig. 8).
65: <header>3.2.7. Egg breaking and further processing facilities </header>

Similar to shell egg processing and packaging, the share of supply chain resource use and emissions attributable to activities in egg breaking and further processing facilities is small. Eggs contribute the majority of resource use and emissions across all impact categories considered. Direct water use accounts for 12% of overall water use, and packaging for pasteurized, liquid whole egg products contributes 7% of supply chain GHG emissions, 17% of water use and 31% of energy use (Table S36, Fig. 9).
66: <header>3.2.8. Transportation of eggs and egg products to consumers </header>

The distances that eggs and egg products may be transported to customers, as well as the mode of transport, may vary widely. The potential relevance of transporting eggs and egg products for overall supply chain resource use and emissions is demonstrated using packaged shell eggs assumed to be transported 100 km, 500 km or 1000 km by truck. Table S37 and Fig. 10 show the extent to which transport distance influences overall cradle-to-customer resource use and emissions for packaged shell eggs. Trans- portation related impacts are negligible for acidifying and eutro- phying emissions as well as for land and water use. For GHG emissions, overall emissions are only increased by 4% under the longest-distance scenario. For energy use, the transportation phase is slightly more influential, contributing as much as an additional 10% to supply chain energy use under the 1000 km transport dis- tance scenario (Fig. 10).
67: <header>3.2.9. Scenarios for mitigating impacts of egg production </header>

All three scenarios result in marked improvements in the resource and environmental performance profile of egg production. Scenarios 1 (achieving best reported feed conversion efficiency and rate-of-lay) and 2 (least environmental cost feed sourcing) provide for very similar reductions in acidifying and eutrophying emissions compared to the baseline conventional cage system (23-27%). The mitigation potential of Scenario 1 is slightly more modest for greenhouse gas emissions and resource use (13-17%), while Sce- nario 2 shows a stronger mitigation potential (23-34%). In com- bination (Scenario 3), these performance improvement variables demonstrate the potential for considerable reductions in resource use and emissions intensity (i.e. 40% reduction of acidifying emis- sions, 44% for eutrophying emissions, 43% for GHG emissions, and 42%, 32% and 50% for land, water and energy use respectively) (Table S38, Fig. 11).
68: In general, making direct comparisons of results produced by different life cycle assessment studies is inadvisable. This is because differences in results between studies of otherwise similar product systems may reflect differences in methodological choices, as- sumptions and data rather than actual differences in resource use and emissions profiles between the products considered. For this reason, only comparisons between studies based on identical methods may be considered robust.

Pelletier and colleagues recently published an environmental life cycle assessment study of national average egg production in conventional cage systems in the United States in 2010 using the same methods that were employed in the current analysis. This commonality allows for direct comparison of estimated resource use and emissions for Canadian and American eggs for acidifying, eutrophying and GHG emissions, as well as energy use (water and land use were not evaluated in the US study.) Table 4 presents a comparison of estimated resource use and emissions for Canadian and American national average egg production. In general, the observed environmental performance profiles are very similar be- tween the two countries. Estimated emissions are slightly higher per tonne of eggs produced in Canada, whereas estimated supply chain energy use is slightly lower.

Although direct comparisons are challenging, it is nonetheless interesting to note broad similarities and differences in the results of the current study from those reported elsewhere in terms of trends in observed differences in the environmental impacts of egg production by housing system type as well as key contributing aspects. Comparing conventional cage with aviary systems in the Netherlands, Mollenhorst et al. (2006) found that conventional cage production performed better for the environmental variables considered. This finding corresponds reasonably well with the current study, where impacts for free run production were slightly higher than those for conventional cage production in all but one of the resource use and emissions categories considered. In a similar study of conventional cage and free range egg production in Australia, Wiedemann and McGahan (2011) also found lower im- pacts for conventional cage systems.

A UK study comparing conventional cage, free run, free range and organic egg production found that impacts were highest for organic production and lowest for cage production, larger due to lower productivity and feed conversion efficiency in the organic system (Leinonen et al., 2012). This contrasts with the results of the current analysis, where impacts for organic production were lowest. It should be noted that this latter, top-down study was not based on a large, directly collected data set. Producer-reported life cycle inventory data for Canadian production systems indicated quite similar feed conversion efficiencies for conventional cage and organic egg production, with observed differences in estimated supply chain resource use and emissions largely attributable to the lower impacts associated with organic feed production. Indeed, organic systems had slightly lower feed conversion efficiencies and much higher mortality rates in the current analysis.

With respect to the latter observation, it should also be noted that feed inputs derived from animal processing co-products are either not allowed for use in the other countries where LCA studies of eggs were conducted (Netherlands, UK), or these were simply

not included in the modeled feeds (Australia). These materials make substantial contributions to the estimated resource use and emissions associated with conventional feeds used in the Canadian egg supply chain.

Also pertinent is a recent study of egg production in the prov- ince of Alberta, which included an evaluation of conventional cage compared to enriched cage production (Courchesne et al., 2013). Here, it was found that impacts for enriched cage production were slightly lower than those for conventional cage production. Feed conversion efficiencies were similar, but energy use in enriched cage production was lower. This contrasts with the results of the current study, which suggest higher energy use and slightly poorer feed conversion efficiencies for enriched cage production. It should be noted, however, that the Alberta study assumed differences at the inventory level between conventional and enriched systems based on anecdotal reports, whereas the current study is based on directly collected data. It should also be noted that studies in Europe, where enriched production is much more widespread, suggest lower impacts for enriched production. This is likely due to a higher degree of experience and optimization with enriched systems in Europe compared to Canada, where levels of enriched production are still very low but will likely increase substantially in the short to medium term.

74: This cradle-to-customer environmental life cycle assessment study of Canadian eggs and egg products provides a wealth of in- formation that may support continued efforts to understand and improve the environmental sustainability of the Canadian egg in- dustry. In particular, initiatives to improve supply chain resource efficiencies and to reduce the emissions intensity of activities associated with the egg industry can draw directly on the results of this study.

Similar to studies of egg production systems in other jurisdic- tion, it was found that the cradle-to-consumer resource use and emissions attributable to eggs and egg products are largely deter- mined by a relatively small number of variables. Foremost among these is the composition and amount of feed consumed in breeder flock, pullet and layer facilities. In general, feeds that are low in or lacking materials derived from livestock processing co-products have considerably lower impacts. Among all feed inputs sourced along egg supply chains, materials derived from cattle are the most resource and emissions intensive, followed by those from swine and poultry. This intuitive finding simply reflects the multiplier effect for resources and emissions associated with providing the feeds and other inputs to produce and process livestock, as well as render the processing co-products into feed inputs such as tallow, meat and bone meal, and feather meal. At the other end of the spectrum, agricultural crop products used as feed inputs are much less impactful - particularly in the case of legumes like peas and soy, which fix their own nitrogen in place of relying on synthetic nitrogen fertilizer. This latter consideration is among the key determining factors for the resource and emissions intensities of crop production. It is also the most influential factor in the superior resource (other than land use) and environmental performance of organic compared to equivalent conventionally-produced crop
75: This cradle-to-customer environmental life cycle assessment study of Canadian eggs and egg products provides a wealth of in- formation that may support continued efforts to understand and improve the environmental sustainability of the Canadian egg in- dustry. In particular, initiatives to improve supply chain resource efficiencies and to reduce the emissions intensity of activities associated with the egg industry can draw directly on the results of this study.

Similar to studies of egg production systems in other jurisdic- tion, it was found that the cradle-to-consumer resource use and emissions attributable to eggs and egg products are largely deter- mined by a relatively small number of variables. Foremost among these is the composition and amount of feed consumed in breeder flock, pullet and layer facilities. In general, feeds that are low in or lacking materials derived from livestock processing co-products have considerably lower impacts. Among all feed inputs sourced along egg supply chains, materials derived from cattle are the most resource and emissions intensive, followed by those from swine and poultry. This intuitive finding simply reflects the multiplier effect for resources and emissions associated with providing the feeds and other inputs to produce and process livestock, as well as render the processing co-products into feed inputs such as tallow, meat and bone meal, and feather meal. At the other end of the spectrum, agricultural crop products used as feed inputs are much less impactful - particularly in the case of legumes like peas and soy, which fix their own nitrogen in place of relying on synthetic nitrogen fertilizer. This latter consideration is among the key determining factors for the resource and emissions intensities of crop production. It is also the most influential factor in the superior resource (other than land use) and environmental performance of organic compared to equivalent conventionally-produced crop
76: Comparative acidifying, eutrophying and GHG emissions as well as energy use for national average egg production in the United States in 2010 and Canada in 2012.
|Acidifying Emissions kg SO2 eq|Eutrophying Emissions kg PO4- eq|GHG Emissions kg CO2 eq|Energy Use MJ Canada|78|24|2420|11223 United States|70|20|2080|12000

products. These findings explain the wide range in resource use and emissions intensities that were observed for the conventional and organic feeds used in Canadian breeder flock, pullet and layer facilities.

After least environmental cost feed sourcing, the second critical determinant of supply emissions is manure management - in particular for emissions of acidifying and eutrophying compounds. These emissions are influenced by feed composition (i.e. N and P content of feed inputs), feed conversion efficiencies, and manure handling strategies. Variables such as how and for how long manure is stored, whether the storage facility is covered or un- covered, whether liquid or solid manure handing is employed, and how manure is applied to agricultural land (for example, injection versus spreading) will all play a role in determining how much and in what forms nutrients are lost to air, soil and water. Industry-level initiatives focused on optimizing nutrient cycling and minimizing losses of N and P may potentially leverage significant improve- ments in the overall environmental profile of the Canadian egg industry.

Although more strongly influenced by supply chain feed inputs, direct water and energy use in facilities also make non-trivial contributions to overall water and energy use. Moreover, the high degree of variability in reported water and energy use between facilities at each supply chain stage points towards improvement opportunities relative to industry average and best reported per- formance benchmarks.

Among the five housing technologies considered (i.e. conven- tional cage, enriched cage, free run, free range, and organic), both the life cycle inventory and impact assessment results suggested very similar levels of performance between the non-organic sys- items. Only for organic production were resource use and emissions significantly different from the other housing systems. Here, the much lower observed resource use and emissions intensity of organic eggs is largely explained by the superior environmental performance of organic feeds compared to conventional feeds - in particular, those that contain materials derived from livestock - rather than egg farm-level differences, where organic farms actu- ally evince lower performance for most variables considered.

Shell egg processing and packaging, as well as egg breaking and further processing activities add only small increments to the overall supply chain resource use and emissions associated with eggs and egg products. The kinds and amounts of packaging used for egg products may, however, present improvement opportu- nities, as these variables influence GHG emissions, water and en- ergy use to a modest degree. In particular, implementing reusable packaging solutions might be considered.

Transportation of eggs and egg products to customers is simi- larly of modest concern in terms of supply chain resource use and emissions. For example, it was estimated that transporting pro- cessed, packaged shell eggs 1000 km by truck to retail outlets increased overall GHG emissions by only 4% and energy use by 10% compared to products at the facility gate.

As the first national average benchmark study of Canadian egg supply chains and layer housing systems, the study offers insights as to where and to what extent land, water, and energy resources, as well as emissions of greenhouse gases, acidifying and eutro- phying compounds, are concentrated along egg supply chains. It also demonstrates the variability in resource and emissions in- tensities between what are otherwise similar facilities (i.e. providing the same services) at each supply chain stage. For example, the rich survey data set that was collected from industry participants in order to develop the life cycle inventory models indicates considerable gaps between the leaders and laggers in terms of environmental performance profiles. This benchmark data will hence allow supply chain participants to gauge their individual

performance, to understand how and to what extent they might improve relative to the industry average benchmarks as well as best reported performance, and to undertake mitigation activities accordingly. It will also support industry associations and other stakeholders in developing targeted initiatives at the sectoral level.

The resource and environmental performance profile of national average egg production in Canada is very similar to that observed in a recent study of US national average egg production. While emission intensities are slightly higher for Canadian eggs, supply chain energy use is marginally lower. In light of the quite different structure of the Canadian egg industry, which is characterized by numerous smaller-scale producers compared to the much more consolidated and vertically integrated US industry, this level of performance suggests already diligent efforts among Canadian egg farmers to ensure optimal production efficiencies. At the same time, scenarios modeled in this study to assess the mitigation po- tential of achieving best reported performance for feed conversion efficiency and rate-of-lay, as well as sourcing organic feeds, suggest that further reducing the resource and emissions intensity of Ca- nadian egg products by over 50% may be possible.

In conclusion, this study points toward considerable opportu- nities for the Canadian egg industry. The study results should be seen as providing inspiration and sign posts for initiatives intended to continuously improve the sustainability of Canadian eggs and egg products.
